title: Smartphones For Young Children
prompt_text: Some people believe that young children should be allowed to use smartphones because the devices can help them learn and stay connected. Others think children are too young for smartphones and should spend their time on other activities. Do you agree or disagree that young children should be allowed to use smartphones? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position clearly, then give reason one with a concrete example, add reason two with another example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, and finish by restating your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, young children should not be allowed to use smartphones freely. First of all, children at that age are still developing self-control, and smartphones are extremely hard to put down. For instance, my little nephew used to cry every time my sister took the phone away from him, which showed me how quickly it became an addiction. Secondly, too much screen time can hurt their attention span and physical health. Instead of running around outside or reading picture books, they end up sitting still for hours, and that simply isn't healthy for a growing child. On the other hand, I do understand that some educational apps can be useful, and a phone is handy for emergencies. However, I think those benefits can easily come from a parent's phone under close supervision, rather than giving a young child their own device. All things considered, I firmly believe that childhood should be about play, social interaction, and exploring the real world, so smartphones should wait until kids are much older.

title: Social Media And Friendships
prompt_text: Some people think that social media does more harm than good to friendships because it replaces real conversations with online interactions. Others believe social media helps friends stay close, no matter how far apart they live. Do you think social media does more harm than good to friendships? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose a side, support it with two reasons and real examples, then acknowledge the other viewpoint before restating your final position.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I believe social media does more good than harm to friendships, as long as it's used wisely. To begin with, it lets us stay connected with people who live far away. For example, my best friend moved to another country two years ago, and thanks to video calls and messaging, it honestly feels like she never left. We chat almost every day, which would have been impossible in the past. Secondly, social media makes it easy to share little moments and celebrate good news instantly, so friendships feel alive even between meetups. However, I do admit there's a downside. Some people fall into the comparison trap, measuring their lives against the perfect images they see online, and that can create jealousy or make friendships feel superficial. Still, I'd argue that depends on the person, not the technology itself. To sum up, while social media certainly has its risks, I'm convinced that overall it strengthens friendships by keeping us close, and that's why I see it as a positive force.

title: Will AI Improve Daily Life
prompt_text: Some people are optimistic that artificial intelligence will improve everyday life by making tasks faster and easier. Others worry that it will create new problems and take away jobs. Do you agree or disagree that artificial intelligence will improve everyday life overall? Give reasons and examples to support your view.
template_hint: Take a clear stance, defend it with two well-developed reasons and examples, concede a valid counterpoint, then circle back to reaffirm your opinion.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that artificial intelligence will improve everyday life overall. First and foremost, AI saves us an enormous amount of time on routine tasks. Take navigation apps, for example. They use AI to predict traffic and reroute us instantly, so a commute that once took an hour might now take forty minutes. That convenience adds up across millions of people every single day. Secondly, AI is making important services more accessible. In healthcare, for instance, AI tools can help doctors spot diseases in scans earlier and more accurately, which can genuinely save lives. Admittedly, I understand the concern that AI might replace certain jobs, and that's a real challenge society will have to manage carefully. Nevertheless, history shows that new technology usually creates fresh kinds of work even as it removes old ones. In conclusion, while we should stay thoughtful about the risks, I'm optimistic that, looking at the bigger picture, artificial intelligence will make our lives more efficient, safer, and ultimately better for most people.

title: Relying Too Much On Technology
prompt_text: Some people feel that we rely too much on technology in our daily lives and are losing basic skills as a result. Others argue that depending on technology is simply a smart way to live in the modern world. Do you agree that people rely too much on technology in daily life? Give reasons and examples to explain your opinion.
template_hint: Declare your opinion up front, back it with two reasons illustrated by examples, recognize the opposing argument, and end with a clear restatement.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, people do rely too much on technology in their daily lives. For one thing, many of us have lost basic skills that used to be second nature. A good example is directions. I have friends who can't drive even five minutes without their GPS, and if their battery dies, they feel completely helpless in their own city. For another, technology is weakening our memory. We no longer bother to remember phone numbers or even simple facts because we assume we can just look everything up in seconds. Granted, I'll admit that technology is incredibly useful and saves us a lot of time, so I'm not saying we should throw it all away. That being said, the problem is balance. When we depend on devices for absolutely everything, we stop exercising our own minds and abilities. Overall, I strongly feel that technology should be a helpful tool we fall back on, not a crutch we can't live without, and right now too many people have crossed that line.

title: Online Versus Face-To-Face Communication
prompt_text: Some people believe that communicating online through texts and video calls is just as meaningful as talking face-to-face. Others feel that nothing can replace being together in person. Which do you think is more meaningful, online communication or face-to-face communication? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
connector_phrases: When all is said and done
template_hint: Pick the option you prefer, justify it with two reasons and vivid examples, fairly acknowledge the alternative, then restate which one you find more meaningful.
sample_response_band12: If you ask me, face-to-face communication is far more meaningful than communicating online. To start with, when we're together in person, we can read body language and tone of voice, which carry so much of what we really mean. Just imagine comforting a friend who is upset. A warm hug or a hand on the shoulder says more than any text message ever could. Secondly, being physically present removes a lot of the misunderstandings that happen online. A short message can easily sound cold or rude when the person didn't mean it that way at all, and these little misreadings can damage relationships. While it's true that online communication is incredibly convenient and helps us stay in touch across long distances, I'd argue it's more of a backup than a real substitute. Even so, it often feels rushed and a bit empty compared to a genuine, heartfelt conversation. When all is said and done, I believe that nothing can replace the warmth and connection of talking to someone face-to-face, and that's why I value it most.

title: Should Everyone Learn Programming
prompt_text: Some people argue that everyone should learn basic computer programming because it is becoming an essential skill in the modern world. Others believe that programming is only necessary for those who work in technology. Do you agree or disagree that everyone should learn basic computer programming? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you agree, develop two supporting reasons with examples, give credit to the other side, and conclude by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: In my view, everyone should learn at least basic computer programming. First of all, programming teaches a way of thinking that's useful in almost any job. It trains you to break big problems into small steps and to think logically, and those problem-solving skills carry over into everyday life. Consider, for example, a manager who understands a little code. She can communicate far better with her tech team and make smarter decisions. Secondly, we live in such a tech-driven world that basic digital literacy is becoming as fundamental as reading or math. Even simple coding helps people understand how the apps and websites they use every day actually work. Now, I can see why some people argue that programming isn't for everyone and that not every career requires it. Even so, I'm not suggesting everyone become a software engineer, just that everyone learn the basics. Ultimately, I firmly believe that a little programming knowledge empowers people, opens up opportunities, and helps them feel confident in a world that runs increasingly on technology, so it's well worth learning for all of us.

title: Self-Checkout and Shopping Experience
prompt_text: Many stores now use automated systems such as self-checkout machines instead of cashiers. Some people think this makes shopping faster and more convenient, while others feel it makes the experience worse and less personal. Do you think automation like self-checkout improves the shopping experience? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Open with your opinion, support it with two reasons and concrete examples, acknowledge one drawback from the other side, and close by restating your view.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I think automation like self-checkout does improve the shopping experience overall. The main reason is that it saves a lot of time. Let me give an example. When I only have a few items, I really don't want to wait in a long queue behind someone with a full cart. With self-checkout, I can scan my groceries, tap my card, and be out the door in under two minutes. Another reason is that it gives shoppers more control and a bit of privacy. I can take my time bagging things the way I like without feeling rushed by a cashier or the people behind me. Sure, I'll admit there are downsides. The machines sometimes glitch, and the experience can feel a little impersonal compared to chatting with a friendly cashier. Then again, most stores still keep a few staff nearby to help when something goes wrong, so you're never truly stuck. All in all, I believe the speed and convenience clearly outweigh the negatives, and that's why I think self-checkout makes shopping better.

title: Reducing Or Eliminating Homework
prompt_text: Some people believe that homework should be greatly reduced or even eliminated, arguing that students need more time to rest and pursue other interests. Others think that homework is essential because it reinforces what is taught in class. Do you agree or disagree that homework should be greatly reduced or eliminated? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position clearly, then give two reasons each supported by a concrete example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, and finish by restating your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, homework should be reduced, but not eliminated completely. First of all, many students today are simply overloaded. When I was in high school, I would finish class and then spend four or five hours on assignments every night, which left me exhausted and even a little anxious. Cutting that down would give students time to sleep, exercise, and spend time with family, all of which matter for healthy development. Secondly, a smaller amount of well-chosen homework is actually more effective. For instance, ten focused math problems teach far more than fifty repetitive ones, because students stay engaged instead of rushing through. Now, I understand the other side. Some people argue that homework builds self-discipline and helps students retain what they learned in class, and there's truth to that. That's exactly why I wouldn't get rid of it entirely. All things considered, I firmly believe schools should keep a light, meaningful amount of homework while dropping the busywork. That balance respects students' time and still reinforces their learning, which is the best outcome for everyone.

title: Is A University Degree Necessary
prompt_text: Some people believe that a university degree is necessary for a successful career, while others argue that skills and experience matter more than formal qualifications. Do you think a university degree is necessary to have a successful career? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Take a clear side, support it with two reasons and concrete examples, recognize the opposing viewpoint, and close by reaffirming your position.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I believe a university degree is not strictly necessary for a successful career, although it certainly helps in some fields. To begin with, many of today's most valuable skills can be learned outside a classroom. For example, my cousin never attended university, but he taught himself web development through online courses and now earns more than several of my degree-holding friends. His success came from real, hands-on experience and a strong portfolio, not a diploma. In addition, a degree can be expensive and time-consuming, leaving graduates with debt and no guaranteed job in a competitive market. However, I do recognize the other side. In professions like medicine, law, or engineering, a degree isn't just useful, it's absolutely required, and rightly so, because public safety depends on it. So I'm not saying education doesn't matter. To sum up, I firmly believe that success depends far more on a person's skills, determination, and willingness to keep learning than on whether they hold a university degree. The right path simply depends on the career someone chooses to pursue.

title: Online Versus Classroom Learning
prompt_text: These days, students can learn either online or in a traditional physical classroom. Some people think students learn better online because of the flexibility it offers, while others believe a physical classroom is more effective for real learning. Which do you think is better for students, learning online or learning in a classroom? Give reasons and examples for your opinion.
template_hint: Choose one option, justify it with two reasons backed by examples, fairly acknowledge the alternative, and conclude by restating which you prefer.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, students generally learn better in a physical classroom, even though online learning has clear advantages. First and foremost, a classroom offers face-to-face interaction that's hard to replace. Take, for example, a student who's confused during a lesson. In person, the teacher can read their expression, pause, and explain it a different way immediately, whereas online that confusion often goes unnoticed. Moreover, a classroom keeps students focused and accountable. When I tried online courses during the pandemic, I was constantly distracted by my phone and household noise, and my grades slipped because nobody was there to keep me on track. That said, I do appreciate online learning's flexibility, especially for working adults or people in remote areas who otherwise couldn't attend school at all. It genuinely opens doors. In conclusion, while online education is a valuable option, I firmly believe most students learn more effectively in a physical classroom, because the interaction, structure, and human connection it provides lead to deeper understanding and stronger results overall.

title: Teaching Practical Life Skills
prompt_text: Some people believe that schools should teach more practical life skills, such as managing money, cooking, and basic repairs, instead of focusing only on academic subjects. Others feel that schools should concentrate on traditional subjects like math, science, and literature. Do you agree or disagree that schools should teach more practical life skills? Support your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Declare your position, develop two reasons each with a real example, briefly address the other viewpoint, and end by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that schools should teach more practical life skills, because students need to be ready for the real world, not just for exams. To start with, many young people graduate without knowing how to manage money. A good example is budgeting and understanding credit cards. I have friends who left school as straight-A students yet fell into debt within a year because nobody ever taught them financial literacy. That's a serious gap. Besides that, basic skills like cooking, doing laundry, or handling small repairs make people far more independent when they move out on their own. I learned to cook from videos, not from school, and I wish that had been taught earlier. On the other hand, I do understand that academic subjects matter and shouldn't be replaced, since they build critical thinking and open career doors. So I'm not suggesting we cut math or science. All in all, I firmly believe schools should add practical life skills alongside traditional subjects, because preparing students for everyday life is just as valuable as preparing them for university.

title: Do Final Exams Measure Learning
prompt_text: Some people believe that final exams fairly measure what students have learned over a course, while others argue that a single exam cannot truly reflect a student's knowledge or abilities. Do you think final exams are a fair way to measure what students have learned? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you find exams fair, give two supporting reasons with examples, concede a point to the other side, and finish with a firm conclusion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, final exams are not a completely fair way to measure what students have learned, even though they do have some value. For one thing, a single exam mostly rewards memorization rather than genuine understanding. To illustrate, I once crammed an entire history course the night before the test, scored well, and then forgot almost everything within a week. That high mark didn't reflect any real, lasting knowledge. Furthermore, exams put enormous pressure on students, and test anxiety can cause even well-prepared people to perform poorly. A friend of mine understood the material deeply but froze during the final and got a grade that simply didn't represent her true ability. Admittedly, exams do have advantages. They're consistent, they cover a lot of material at once, and they're hard to cheat on, which is why schools rely on them. Ultimately, though, I firmly believe exams alone aren't enough. A fairer system would combine exams with projects and ongoing assessments, because only a mix of methods can truly capture what a student has actually learned.

title: Should Second Languages Be Required
prompt_text: Some people believe that learning a second language should be a required subject in school, while others think students should be free to choose whether to study one. Do you agree or disagree that a second language should be mandatory in school? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Pick a side, back it with two reasons illustrated by examples, acknowledge the opposing argument, and close by restating your view.
sample_response_band12: I'm of the opinion that learning a second language should be required in school, because the benefits go far beyond simply speaking another language. First of all, studying a new language broadens students' horizons and builds cultural awareness. A clear example is how learning French in school made me curious about other cultures and far more comfortable interacting with people from different backgrounds. On top of that, research shows real cognitive benefits. Bilingual people often have stronger memory and better problem-solving skills, which help in every subject, not just language class. There's also a practical side. In today's global economy, speaking two languages can open doors to jobs that monolingual candidates simply can't reach. Having said that, I do understand the concern that forcing a language on uninterested students might be ineffective, and some learners would rather focus elsewhere. That's a fair point. In the end, though, I firmly believe a second language should be mandatory, at least in the early years, because the long-term advantages for students' minds and futures are simply too valuable to leave to chance.

title: Later Start To The School Day
prompt_text: Some people believe that the school day should start later in the morning so that students can get more sleep and concentrate better. Others argue that an early start is better because it prepares students for the working world and frees up the afternoon. Do you agree or disagree that the school day should start later in the morning? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Make your position clear, support it with two reasons and concrete examples, fairly note the opposing argument, and end by restating your opinion.
sample_response_band12: As far as I'm concerned, the school day should start later in the morning, mainly because so many students are simply not getting enough sleep. To begin with, teenagers naturally tend to fall asleep late and need more rest than adults, yet most schools force them to wake up before sunrise. Consider, for instance, my own high school experience. Our first class began at seven thirty, and I often sat there half-asleep, unable to absorb anything until mid-morning. A later start would mean students arrive well-rested and ready to learn. What's more, several studies have linked later start times to better academic performance and even improved mental health, which is hard to ignore. Granted, I understand the opposing view. An early start frees up the afternoon for sports, jobs, and family time, and it does mirror a typical work schedule. Those are reasonable points. On the whole, though, I firmly believe the benefits of more sleep outweigh the inconvenience. Even shifting the start by an hour could help students concentrate better, feel healthier, and ultimately get more out of their education, which should be the real priority.

title: Is A Four-Day Work Week Good?
prompt_text: Some companies are considering switching to a four-day work week instead of the traditional five days. Some people believe this change would make employees happier and more productive, while others worry that important work would not get done. Do you think a four-day work week is a good idea? Give reasons and examples to explain your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you support a four-day week, give reason one (e.g., better rest) with an example, give reason two (e.g., higher focus) with an example, briefly acknowledge the workload concern, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, a four-day work week is definitely a good idea, and I'd love to see more companies try it. First of all, I think it would dramatically improve people's work-life balance. For instance, my cousin works long hours and barely has time for her kids, so an extra day off would let her actually rest and recharge instead of running on empty. On top of that, I genuinely believe people would be more productive, not less. When I have a clear deadline and limited time, I focus much harder and waste less time on distractions like checking my phone. Of course, I understand the worry that some important work might not get finished, especially in industries like healthcare where people are needed every day. But for most office jobs, I think companies could easily reorganize tasks and still meet their goals. All things considered, a four-day work week would boost morale, reduce burnout, and probably make employees more loyal. That's why I firmly believe it's a smart move that more businesses should seriously consider trying out.

title: Follow Your Passion Or A Stable Job
prompt_text: When choosing a career, some people believe you should follow your passion and do what you love. Others think it is wiser to choose a stable job that offers a reliable income. Which do you think is the better approach? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose passion or stability, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the appeal of the other option, then conclude by restating your choice.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I believe it's better to follow your passion when choosing a career. To begin with, when you genuinely love what you do, you stay motivated even when things get difficult. For example, my friend always dreamed of becoming a chef, and even though the hours are brutal, he wakes up excited every single day, which is something money simply can't buy. What's more, I think passion often leads to success in the long run. When you care deeply about your work, you naturally improve faster and people notice that dedication, which can eventually lead to better pay anyway. That being said, I completely understand why some people prioritize a stable job. Financial security matters, especially if you have a family to support, and not everyone can afford to take risks. However, I'd rather find a way to combine both than settle for a job that bores me for forty years. In conclusion, while stability is important, I firmly believe that following your passion makes life far more fulfilling, and that long-term happiness is worth pursuing.

title: Working From Home Versus The Office
prompt_text: These days, many people have the choice between working from home and working in a traditional office. Some people prefer the flexibility of working from home, while others feel they are more focused and connected in an office. Which do you think is better? Give reasons and examples for your opinion.
template_hint: State your preference, give reason one (e.g., flexibility or focus) with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge a benefit of the opposite option, then restate your view.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that working from home is better than working in an office, at least for most jobs. For one thing, it gives you incredible flexibility over your day. For instance, instead of wasting two hours stuck in traffic, I can use that time to exercise or simply start working earlier, which makes me far more efficient. In addition, I find I'm actually more focused at home because there are fewer interruptions. In an office, coworkers constantly stop by my desk to chat, but at home I can put my head down and finish a task in half the time. Although I do recognize the downsides, especially the risk of feeling isolated and missing out on face-to-face collaboration, I think these problems can easily be solved with video calls and occasional team meetups. Honestly, the freedom and comfort of working from home outweigh those concerns for me. To sum up, while the office certainly has its advantages, I firmly believe working from home leads to a better work-life balance and higher productivity, which is why I would choose it every time.

title: Is Changing Jobs Frequently Good?
prompt_text: Some people believe that changing jobs frequently is good for your career because it brings new skills and higher pay. Others think it is better to stay with one company for a long time to build loyalty and experience. What is your opinion? Support your answer with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Take a side on frequent job changes, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the value of the opposite approach, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, changing jobs fairly often, though not too often, can be very good for your career. First and foremost, moving between companies exposes you to new skills and fresh ideas. Take, for example, a marketing specialist who works at three different firms in five years; she'll learn three different ways of doing things and become far more adaptable than someone who's only ever seen one company. Furthermore, switching jobs is often the fastest way to increase your salary. In my experience, employers tend to offer bigger raises to attract new talent than they give to loyal employees, so staying put can actually cost you money. On the flip side, I do understand the value of loyalty. Sticking with one company lets you build deep relationships and a strong reputation, and changing jobs too frequently can make you look unreliable. Ultimately, though, I believe a healthy amount of job changing keeps you out of your comfort zone and accelerates your growth. That's why I think it's generally a smart move for an ambitious person.

title: Ignoring Work Messages After Hours
prompt_text: Some people believe that employees should have the right to ignore work emails and messages once their workday is over. Others feel that staying available after hours is simply part of a modern job. Do you think employees should be free to ignore work messages after hours? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether employees should be able to ignore after-hours messages, give reason one with an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that employees should be free to ignore work messages once their workday is over. To start with, everyone needs personal time to rest and recharge. For example, if a manager texts you about a report at nine in the evening while you're having dinner with your family, answering it steals time that should belong entirely to you and your loved ones. Moreover, constant availability creates serious stress and eventually leads to burnout. I've seen friends who feel they can never truly relax because their phones might buzz at any moment, and that kind of pressure damages both their health and their performance at work. Admittedly, there are certain urgent jobs, like doctors or emergency staff, where being reachable is genuinely necessary, and I respect that. However, for the vast majority of regular office jobs, most messages can easily wait until the next morning. In conclusion, I strongly feel that setting clear boundaries between work and personal life makes employees happier, healthier, and ultimately more productive, which is why I believe people should absolutely have the right to disconnect after hours.

title: Should Companies Offer Unlimited Vacation?
prompt_text: Some companies have started offering employees unlimited paid vacation, letting them take as many days off as they want. Some people think this builds trust and reduces stress, while others worry it could be abused or actually discourage people from taking time off. Do you think companies should offer unlimited paid vacation? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position on unlimited vacation, give your main reason with an example, add a second reason with an example, acknowledge a downside, then restate your view.
sample_response_band12: In my view, companies should offer unlimited paid vacation, although it has to be managed carefully. The main reason is that it shows employees they are trusted as responsible adults. To illustrate, a friend of mine works at a startup with this policy, and she says feeling trusted makes her work even harder, because she doesn't want to let her team down. Besides that, unlimited vacation gives people the flexibility to rest exactly when they need it, which reduces stress and prevents burnout. For instance, if someone is exhausted after a huge project, they can take a few extra days and come back genuinely refreshed instead of dragging themselves through the office. Having said that, I do recognize the risks. Some people might take advantage of the system, and ironically, others might feel peer pressure and end up taking fewer days than before. That's why clear expectations and good management are essential. All in all, though, I believe the benefits of trust, flexibility, and lower stress clearly outweigh the drawbacks, so it's a forward-thinking policy that more companies should adopt.

title: Pressure Or Flexible Deadlines
prompt_text: People often disagree about how to do their best work. Some believe they perform better under pressure with tight deadlines, while others feel they produce higher-quality work when given flexible deadlines and time to think. Which do you think helps people perform better? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose pressure or flexibility, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing approach, then restate your choice.
sample_response_band12: Speaking for myself, I believe most people actually perform better with flexible deadlines rather than under intense pressure. The first thing is that having enough time allows for higher-quality, more thoughtful work. A good example is writing an essay: when I rush it the night before, it's full of mistakes, but when I have a week, I can revise it carefully and produce something I'm genuinely proud of. What's more, flexibility encourages creativity. Great ideas often come when your mind is relaxed, like in the shower or on a walk, and you simply can't force that kind of inspiration when you're panicking against the clock. On the other hand, I do admit that some pressure can be helpful, because a deadline stops you from procrastinating forever and pushes you to actually finish. A little stress can sharpen your focus. However, too much pressure usually leads to careless errors and burnout. To wrap up, while a small amount of pressure has its place, I firmly believe that reasonable, flexible deadlines bring out people's best work by allowing both careful thinking and genuine creativity.

title: Individual Versus Government On Environment
prompt_text: Some people believe that protecting the environment is mainly the responsibility of individuals, who should change their everyday habits. Others think large organizations and governments should lead the biggest changes. In your opinion, who should be mainly responsible for protecting the environment? Give reasons and examples to support your answer.
template_hint: State whether individuals or larger organizations should be mainly responsible, give reason one with a concrete example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the other side, then restate your position firmly.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, large organizations should be mainly responsible for protecting the environment, although individuals certainly play a part. First of all, big organizations can make changes that affect huge numbers of people at once. For instance, when a company switches its whole delivery fleet to electric vehicles, that single decision cuts far more pollution than thousands of people recycling at home ever could. Secondly, they have the resources to build the systems people need to live greener lives. For example, if there's no proper recycling program or public transit nearby, individuals simply can't make eco-friendly choices, no matter how hard they try. That being said, I do recognize that personal habits matter, and I think we should all reduce waste and save energy where we can. On the other hand, individual effort works much better when the right options actually exist around us. So all things considered, while everyone has a role to play, I firmly believe that the largest organizations must lead the way by investing in green systems and making the sustainable choice the easy, convenient choice for ordinary people like me.

title: Should Single-Use Plastics Be Banned
prompt_text: Many places are considering banning single-use plastics such as straws, bags, and cutlery. Some people support a complete ban to reduce waste, while others worry it would be inconvenient and costly. Do you agree or disagree with banning single-use plastics? Give reasons and examples to explain your opinion.
template_hint: State that you agree or disagree with the ban, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing concern, then conclude by restating your view.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I strongly agree that single-use plastics should be banned. To begin with, these items create an enormous amount of waste that lasts for hundreds of years. For example, a plastic bag is used for maybe ten minutes, but it can sit in the ocean or a landfill for centuries, harming wildlife and ecosystems. What's more, banning them pushes people and businesses toward better alternatives. When my local grocery store stopped offering plastic bags, everyone simply started bringing reusable cloth bags, and honestly, it became a normal habit within weeks. I admit there's an opposing view: some people say a ban is inconvenient and that paper or cloth options cost more. However, I think that small inconvenience is a tiny price to pay compared to the long-term damage plastic does to our planet. Besides, prices usually drop as greener products become more common. In conclusion, the environmental benefits clearly outweigh the minor hassle, so I firmly believe banning single-use plastics is a smart and necessary step for a cleaner, healthier future.

title: Public Transport Or More Roads
prompt_text: Cities have limited budgets and must decide where to spend their money. Some people think cities should invest more in public transport like buses and trains, while others believe building and widening roads is more important. Which do you think cities should prioritize, public transport or roads? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose public transport or roads, give your first reason with an example, give a second reason with an example, acknowledge the alternative briefly, then restate your choice clearly.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that cities should invest more in public transport than in building new roads. First of all, public transport moves far more people in the same amount of space. For instance, a single train can carry hundreds of passengers, while the same number of people in separate cars would jam the streets for kilometres. Adding more roads often just encourages more driving, so the traffic comes back within a few years. In addition, good public transport is better for the environment and for people who can't afford a car. For example, in my city, students and seniors rely heavily on the bus to get to school or medical appointments. On the other hand, I understand that roads still matter for emergency vehicles and deliveries, so we can't ignore them completely. Still, the smarter long-term choice is to give people reliable, affordable alternatives to driving. To sum up, investing in buses and trains reduces congestion, lowers pollution, and helps everyone, which is exactly why I think public transport deserves the larger share of a city's budget.

title: Eating Less Meat For The Planet
prompt_text: It is often said that producing meat uses a lot of land, water, and energy. Some people believe everyone should eat less meat to help protect the planet, while others feel that diet is a personal choice that shouldn't be limited. Do you agree that people should eat less meat to help the environment? Give reasons and examples for your answer.
template_hint: State whether you agree people should eat less meat, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the personal-choice argument, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, people should eat less meat to help protect the planet, even if they don't give it up entirely. To start with, raising animals for food uses a huge amount of resources. For example, producing a single beef burger requires far more water and land than growing the vegetables for a whole salad, and livestock also release a lot of greenhouse gases. So cutting back, even a little, makes a real difference when millions of people do it together. Moreover, eating less meat is often healthier and quite easy to start. For instance, my family began having one or two meat-free days each week, and we barely noticed the change except that our grocery bill dropped. Admittedly, diet is a personal choice, and I'm not saying anyone should be forced to become vegetarian. However, choosing moderation is a simple, practical way to live more sustainably. Overall, I genuinely believe that eating less meat is one of the easiest and most effective steps ordinary people can take to help the environment.

title: Should Fast Fashion Be Discouraged
prompt_text: Fast fashion offers cheap, trendy clothing that changes quickly from season to season. Some people think it should be discouraged because it creates waste and encourages overconsumption, while others value its low prices and variety. Do you think fast fashion should be discouraged? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether fast fashion should be discouraged, give your first reason with an example, give a second reason with an example, concede the affordability point, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: I would argue that fast fashion should definitely be discouraged. First and foremost, it creates a staggering amount of waste. Take, for example, the cheap shirts that fall apart after just a few washes; people throw them out and buy new ones, and mountains of discarded clothing end up in landfills every single year. This throwaway culture simply isn't sustainable. Furthermore, fast fashion pushes people to buy far more than they actually need. For instance, I have friends who order new outfits almost every month just to keep up with trends, and most of those clothes are worn only once or twice. That's a huge waste of money and resources. Granted, I do understand the appeal: fast fashion is genuinely affordable, and not everyone can pay high prices for clothing, which is a fair point. However, I think the real answer is buying fewer, better-quality items that last longer, rather than constantly chasing cheap trends. In the end, because of the environmental harm and the wasteful habits it encourages, I firmly believe fast fashion should be discouraged in favour of more responsible, lasting choices.

title: Required Recycling And Composting
prompt_text: Some communities are considering making recycling and composting mandatory, so every household would be required to sort its waste. Some people support this rule because it reduces garbage, while others feel it should remain a personal choice. Do you think households should be required to recycle and compost? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether recycling and composting should be required, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the personal-choice view, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: I think households should definitely be required to recycle and compost. To begin with, making it mandatory keeps a huge amount of garbage out of landfills. For example, food scraps and paper that get composted or recycled don't sit and rot, releasing harmful gases. When everyone takes part, the impact across a whole city is enormous. Besides, a rule like this helps turn good habits into normal, everyday behaviour. When my neighbourhood introduced separate bins for compost, it felt strange at first, but within a couple of months it became completely automatic, and now nobody even thinks about it. That's the power of a clear rule. Some might say that sorting waste should be a personal choice and that people shouldn't be forced. I understand that view, but honestly, the environment affects everyone, so I don't think it's fair to leave it entirely up to individuals. A gentle requirement, with a little education and support, gets far better results. In conclusion, because it reduces waste and builds lasting habits, I firmly believe households should be required to recycle and compost.

title: Regulating Unhealthy Food And Drinks
prompt_text: Some people believe governments should regulate unhealthy food and drinks by adding taxes or warning labels to products high in sugar, salt, or fat. Others think people should be free to make their own food choices without government involvement. What is your opinion? Give reasons and examples to support your answer.
template_hint: State your position clearly, then give reason one with a concrete example, add reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, and finish by restating your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, governments definitely should regulate unhealthy food and drinks, mainly to protect public health. First of all, many people simply don't know how much sugar or salt is hidden in everyday products, so clear warning labels help them make informed choices. For instance, a can of soda can contain almost ten teaspoons of sugar, and most people would be shocked to see that on the front of the can. Secondly, things like a sugar tax can genuinely change behaviour. In several countries, after a sugar tax was introduced, companies actually reformulated their drinks to contain less sugar, which benefited everyone automatically. Now, I do understand the other side. Some people feel the government shouldn't tell adults what to eat, and I agree that freedom matters. However, regulation isn't a ban; it's just giving people better information and gentle incentives. The final decision still belongs to the consumer. All things considered, when unhealthy diets lead to serious diseases that burden our whole healthcare system, I firmly believe sensible regulation is a reasonable and caring thing for governments to do.

title: Mandatory Daily Exercise In Schools
prompt_text: Some people think schools should require students to do physical activity every single day. Others believe students already have busy schedules and exercise should be optional. Do you agree or disagree that daily physical activity should be mandatory in schools? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether you agree or disagree, support it with two reasons each backed by an example, acknowledge the busy-schedule concern, and conclude by restating your view.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I strongly agree that daily physical activity should be mandatory in schools. To begin with, children today live very sedentary lives because of phones and computers, so school may be their only real chance to move their bodies. For example, a short daily session of running or team games could replace an hour they'd otherwise spend sitting and scrolling. In addition, exercise actually helps students learn better. Research shows that physical activity improves concentration and mood, so a quick burst of movement before class can help kids focus and even reduce stress before exams. Of course, some people argue that students are already busy and shouldn't be forced. That's a fair point, but we're only talking about twenty or thirty minutes a day, which is a tiny part of the schedule. Making it mandatory also builds a lifelong habit, so children grow into healthy adults. To sum up, the benefits for both health and learning are simply too important to leave to chance, so I firmly believe daily physical activity should be a required part of every school day.

title: Paid Mental-Health Days At Work
prompt_text: Some people believe workplaces should offer employees paid days off specifically for their mental health. Others feel that regular sick leave and vacation days are already enough. In your opinion, should workplaces offer paid mental-health days? Give reasons and examples to support your answer.
template_hint: State your position, give two reasons supported by examples, recognize the counterargument about existing leave, and close by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that workplaces should offer paid mental-health days, because looking after the mind is just as important as looking after the body. First and foremost, these days help prevent burnout. When employees feel overwhelmed, a single day to rest and recharge can stop a small problem from turning into a long-term breakdown that keeps them off work for weeks. Take, for example, someone who takes one mental-health day and comes back far more focused and productive than if they had pushed through exhausted. Moreover, offering these days reduces the stigma around mental health. It sends a clear message that the company genuinely cares, which improves loyalty and helps keep good staff. Now, while it's true that workers already get sick days, many people feel guilty using a sick day when they're not physically ill, so they keep struggling instead. A dedicated mental-health day removes that guilt. In conclusion, happier, healthier employees are also more productive employees, so in my view, paid mental-health days are a smart investment for any modern workplace.

title: Relying on Quick Health Fixes
prompt_text: Some people believe that many of us depend too much on quick fixes for our health, such as supplements, fad diets, or weight-loss pills, instead of building good habits. Others think these shortcuts are a convenient and acceptable part of modern life. Do you agree or disagree that people rely too much on quick fixes for their health? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Take a clear position, develop two reasons with concrete examples, concede a point to the other side, and end with a firm restatement of your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, people absolutely do rely too much on quick fixes for their health, and it often does more harm than good. The main reason is that quick fixes treat the symptom, not the cause. A good example is fad diets: someone might lose weight rapidly in two weeks, but because they never changed their everyday eating habits, the weight almost always comes back, sometimes worse than before. Furthermore, this culture of instant gratification creates false expectations. People take a handful of supplements and assume they're covered, while ignoring the boring but essential basics like sleep, balanced meals, and regular movement. No pill can replace the benefits of simply walking every day. Admittedly, some shortcuts have their place; a busy person might genuinely need a vitamin if their diet is lacking, and that's perfectly reasonable. However, that should support good habits, not replace them. Ultimately, real health comes from small, sustainable choices repeated over years, not from a magic product. So yes, I firmly believe we've become far too dependent on quick fixes, and we'd all be healthier focusing on the fundamentals instead.

title: Do Fitness Trackers Improve Health
prompt_text: Some people believe that fitness trackers and smartwatches genuinely make people healthier by motivating them to move more. Others argue these devices only collect data and don't actually change anyone's behaviour. In your opinion, do fitness trackers really make people healthier? Give reasons and examples to support your answer.
template_hint: State your opinion, back it with two reasons and examples, briefly address the opposing view, then restate your position in a strong conclusion.
sample_response_band12: I'd say that fitness trackers really can make people healthier, as long as they're used in the right way. One key reason is that these devices create accountability. When you can actually see that you've only walked two thousand steps by the afternoon, you feel motivated to get up and reach your goal. To give an example, a friend of mine started parking further from work and taking the stairs purely because her watch kept nudging her to close her daily rings. On top of that, trackers turn invisible progress into something visible and even fun. Seeing your sleep improve or your heart rate drop over time gives a real sense of achievement that keeps people going. However, I do admit they aren't magic. If someone glances at the data and ignores it, the device changes nothing, and a few people even feel anxious from too many notifications. So the tool itself isn't enough; the person has to engage with it. All in all, while a tracker won't do the work for you, I genuinely believe it can be a powerful little motivator that pushes people toward healthier daily habits.

title: Does a Strict Routine Help Wellbeing
prompt_text: Some people believe that following a strict daily routine, with fixed times for waking, eating, working, and sleeping, greatly improves a person's wellbeing. Others think too much routine is restrictive and that flexibility is healthier. Do you agree or disagree that a strict daily routine improves a person's wellbeing? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose a side, give two supporting reasons with examples, acknowledge the value of the opposing view, and finish by restating your position clearly.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, a strict daily routine genuinely does improve a person's wellbeing, because structure brings a sense of calm and control. The first reason is that routines remove decision fatigue. When you already know when you'll wake, eat, and work, you save mental energy for the things that actually matter. For instance, people who keep a consistent sleep schedule tend to sleep better and feel far more rested than those who go to bed at random hours. Beyond that, routines build discipline and momentum. If exercise happens automatically at seven every morning, you don't have to argue with yourself about whether to do it, so good habits stick. Now, it's true that being too rigid can backfire. Life is unpredictable, and someone who panics whenever their schedule breaks may end up more stressed, not less. So I'd say a routine should be firm but not unbreakable, leaving a little room for spontaneity. In the end, though, the stability and predictability of a solid daily routine outweigh the downsides, which is why I firmly believe it makes most people calmer, more productive, and ultimately happier.

title: Sleep Versus Diet And Exercise
prompt_text: Some people believe that getting enough sleep matters more for your health than eating well or exercising. Others think diet and exercise are more important. Which do you think matters most for good health, sleep or diet and exercise? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Pick which factor matters most, give two reasons with examples, acknowledge the importance of the other factors, and close by restating your choice.
sample_response_band12: If you ask me, getting enough sleep matters even more than diet and exercise, because sleep is really the foundation that everything else depends on. To start with, sleep is when the body repairs itself. A clear example is that no matter how hard you train at the gym, your muscles only actually recover and grow while you're asleep, so poor sleep wastes a lot of that effort. What's more, sleep deprivation affects almost everything else. When I'm exhausted, I crave junk food, I skip my workouts, and I can barely concentrate, so one bad night quietly damages my diet and exercise too. In other words, sleep controls the choices that keep us healthy. Of course, I'm not saying diet and exercise don't matter; they absolutely do, and ideally we'd look after all three together. But if I had to rank them, sleep comes first, because without it the other two fall apart. All things considered, I firmly believe good sleep is the single most important pillar of health, and it deserves far more attention than it usually gets.

title: Save Money Or Enjoy Now
prompt_text: Some people believe it is wiser to save most of your money for the future, while others think you should enjoy your money and spend it on experiences now. Which approach do you think is better? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you favour saving or spending, give reason one with a real example, give reason two with an example, briefly admit the value of the other view, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, it's smarter to save most of your money for the future, even though spending now is definitely tempting. First of all, saving gives you real peace of mind. For instance, when my car suddenly broke down last winter, I had a rainy day fund ready, so I fixed it without stress or borrowing from anyone. Secondly, saving lets you reach bigger goals that genuinely change your life. A friend of mine put aside money for three years and finally bought her first apartment, which spending on trips would never have made possible. Now, I do understand the other side. Life is short, and experiences like travelling with family can create memories that money can't replace, so enjoying yourself sometimes is healthy. However, I believe the smartest approach is balance, with savings as the priority. You can still treat yourself while quietly protecting your future. All things considered, putting savings first gives you security today and freedom tomorrow, and that's exactly why I firmly believe it's the wiser choice for most people.

title: Renting Versus Buying a Home
prompt_text: Some people think renting a home gives you flexibility and freedom, while others believe buying a home is a smarter long-term investment. Which do you think is the better choice? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose renting or buying, support it with one financial reason and an example, add a lifestyle reason with an example, acknowledge the opposing benefit, then conclude firmly.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think buying a home is the smarter choice for most people in the long run. To begin with, when you buy, you build equity instead of paying someone else's mortgage. For example, my parents bought their house twenty years ago, and today its value has nearly tripled, giving them real financial security. In addition, owning a home gives you stability and control. You can renovate, paint, or keep a pet without asking a landlord for permission, which makes a house truly feel like yours. However, I do recognize that renting offers flexibility, especially for younger people who change jobs or cities often. A renter can simply move when a better opportunity comes along, with no property to sell. That freedom is genuinely valuable early in life. Even so, once your life is settled, the financial benefits of ownership are hard to ignore. All things considered, I firmly believe buying a home is the wiser long-term decision, because it turns a monthly cost into a lasting investment that can support you for decades.

title: Moving To A Cashless Society
prompt_text: Some people argue that society should move to a fully cashless system where all payments are digital, while others believe physical cash should always remain available. Do you agree or disagree that society should become fully cashless? Give reasons and examples to support your view.
template_hint: Take a clear agree or disagree position, give one practical reason with an example, add a second reason with an example, fairly note the opposing concern, then restate your stance.
sample_response_band12: I firmly disagree that society should become fully cashless, even though digital payments are clearly convenient. First and foremost, cash protects people who are left out of the digital world. For instance, my grandmother doesn't own a smartphone, and in a cashless system she simply couldn't buy her groceries or pay at a local market. Moreover, going fully cashless creates serious risks. When a power outage or system failure happens, digital payments stop working completely, but cash still functions, which is why many small shops keep it as a backup. There's also the issue of privacy, since every digital transaction can be tracked and stored. On the other hand, I do admit that cashless systems are faster, cleaner, and harder for thieves to physically steal, and for everyday shopping they're genuinely efficient. However, convenience shouldn't come at the cost of leaving vulnerable people behind. In conclusion, I believe digital payments should keep growing, but cash must always stay available as a fair and reliable option for everyone, not just the technologically connected.

title: Teaching Money Management In School
prompt_text: Some people believe schools should teach children money management as a required subject, while others think families should handle this at home. Do you think money management should be taught in school? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give one reason about practical benefit with an example, add a fairness or consistency reason with an example, acknowledge the family-teaching view, then conclude.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe schools should teach children money management as a required subject. First of all, it's a practical life skill that everyone needs but few people learn properly. For example, many young adults get their first credit card and quickly fall into debt simply because no one ever taught them how interest works. Furthermore, teaching it in school ensures every child gets the same chance to learn, regardless of their family background. Not all parents understand budgeting or feel comfortable explaining it, so leaving it only to families means some kids are left behind. A short weekly class on saving, spending, and budgeting could prepare students far better for real life. On the other hand, I do understand that families play an important role, since children learn a lot by watching how their parents handle money day to day. However, home lessons alone are inconsistent and not guaranteed. Overall, I firmly believe financial literacy belongs in the school curriculum, because preparing students to manage money responsibly is just as important as teaching them math or reading.

title: Owning Fewer Possessions And Happiness
prompt_text: Some people believe that owning fewer possessions leads to a happier and less stressful life, while others think having more things makes life more comfortable and enjoyable. Which view do you agree with more? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Pick a side, give an emotional or mental reason with an example, add a practical reason with an example, acknowledge the comfort of having things, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, owning fewer possessions generally leads to a happier and calmer life. To start with, having less reduces stress because there is simply less to worry about. For instance, when I decluttered my apartment last year and gave away half my clothes, I felt lighter and could actually find everything I needed in the morning. On top of that, owning fewer things saves money and time. A friend of mine stopped buying gadgets he never used, and now he spends that money on travel and hobbies that genuinely make him happy. There's real freedom in not constantly chasing the next purchase. However, I do recognize the other side. Certain possessions, like a comfortable bed or reliable tools, truly improve daily life, and having some comforts isn't a bad thing at all. The key, I think, is keeping what adds value and letting go of the rest. All in all, I firmly believe that focusing on fewer, more meaningful possessions brings more peace and contentment than constantly accumulating things we don't really need.

title: Are Subscription Services Worth It
prompt_text: These days many products and services, from streaming to software, are sold through monthly subscriptions. Some people feel subscriptions are convenient and good value, while others believe the ongoing costs add up and are not worth it. Do you think subscription services are worth their ongoing cost? Explain your opinion.
template_hint: State whether subscriptions are worth it, give one reason with an example, add a second reason with an example, fairly note the opposite concern, then restate your view.
sample_response_band12: I tend to think subscription services are worth their cost, but only when you actually use them. Firstly, subscriptions offer real convenience and flexibility. For example, instead of paying a large amount upfront for software, I pay a small monthly fee, always get the latest updates, and can cancel anytime if I no longer need it. Secondly, they often give excellent value for money. A single streaming subscription gives my whole family thousands of movies and shows for less than the price of two cinema tickets, which is hard to beat. That said, I completely understand the other side. The ongoing fees quietly add up, and many people forget about subscriptions they never use, wasting money month after month. The danger is paying for five services when you really only enjoy one. In addition, prices tend to rise over time. So my honest view is that subscriptions are worth it if you regularly review them and keep only what you truly value. To conclude, I believe subscriptions can be great value, as long as you stay aware and cancel what you don't use.

title: Replacing Tipping With Fair Wages
prompt_text: Some people believe that tipping should be replaced by fair fixed wages paid directly by employers, while others think tipping rewards good service and should continue. Do you agree or disagree that tipping should be replaced by fair fixed wages? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Take a clear position, give a fairness reason with an example, add a stability reason with an example, acknowledge the reward argument for tipping, then restate your stance.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe tipping should be replaced by fair fixed wages paid by employers. First of all, fixed wages give workers real income stability. For instance, a waiter who relies on tips never knows what he'll earn, since a slow night or bad weather can cut his income dramatically through no fault of his own. A steady salary would let him plan his rent and bills with confidence. Moreover, tipping can be unfair and even uncomfortable. Customers often tip based on mood or appearance rather than actual service, which means equally hard-working staff can be paid very differently. Paying fair wages directly puts that responsibility where it belongs, on the employer. On the other hand, I do understand that tipping can motivate excellent service and lets customers reward someone who goes above and beyond. That personal connection has real value for some people. However, good service should come from fair treatment and professionalism, not from depending on a stranger's generosity. In conclusion, I strongly believe replacing tips with fair fixed wages is the more reliable and respectful system for the workers who deserve it most.

title: Big City Or Small Town
prompt_text: Some people believe that living in a big city is the best choice because of the opportunities and conveniences it offers. Others prefer the quiet, slower pace of a small town. In your opinion, which is a better place to live? Give reasons and examples to support your answer.
template_hint: State which you prefer, give reason one (e.g. opportunities) with an example, give reason two (e.g. convenience) with an example, briefly admit the appeal of the other side, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, a big city is a better place to live, mainly because of the opportunities it offers. First of all, big cities have far more jobs across different industries. For instance, when I was looking for work in marketing, my hometown had almost nothing, but after I moved to Toronto I had three interviews in a single week. Secondly, the convenience is hard to beat. You have public transit, hospitals, restaurants, and shops all within walking distance, so you don't need to plan your whole day around one errand. On top of that, cities are culturally rich, with concerts, museums, and people from all over the world, which I personally find really exciting. Now, I do understand why some people prefer a small town. The pace is slower, the air is cleaner, and you actually know your neighbours, which is genuinely lovely. However, for me, the career growth and everyday convenience clearly outweigh the peace and quiet. All things considered, I firmly believe that a big city gives you more chances to build the life you want.

title: Adults Living With Their Parents
prompt_text: These days, many adults continue to live with their parents instead of moving out on their own. Some people think this is a smart, practical choice, while others believe adults should be independent. Do you think it is a good idea for adults to live with their parents? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason one with an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing view, and finish with a confident restatement.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think it can be a very good idea for adults to live with their parents, at least for a few years. To begin with, it makes a huge financial difference. Rent and groceries are extremely expensive right now, so by staying home, a young adult can save money for something important. For example, my cousin lived with his parents for three years after university and managed to put aside enough for a down payment on his own apartment. Secondly, it keeps families close and allows people to support one another. When my aunt got sick, having her son at home meant someone was always there to help with appointments and meals. However, I do understand the concern that living at home for too long can hold back your independence and make it harder to grow up. That's a fair point. Even so, I believe that as long as the adult contributes, helps out, and has a clear plan, the benefits clearly outweigh the drawbacks. To sum up, living with your parents can be a smart, responsible choice rather than a sign of failure.

title: Limiting Cars Downtown
prompt_text: Some cities are considering limiting the number of cars allowed in their downtown cores in order to reduce traffic and pollution. Supporters say this makes city centres cleaner and safer, while others argue it is inconvenient for drivers and businesses. Do you agree or disagree with limiting cars downtown? Give reasons and examples for your opinion.
template_hint: Take a clear side, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two and an example, acknowledge the inconvenience for the other side, then restate your view.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that cities should limit cars in their downtown cores. First and foremost, fewer cars mean cleaner air and less pollution. For example, when my city closed one main street to traffic on weekends, the air felt noticeably fresher and far more families started spending time downtown. Secondly, limiting cars makes the area much safer and more pleasant for pedestrians and cyclists. Crowded streets with constant traffic are stressful and dangerous, whereas open, walkable spaces encourage people to shop, eat, and relax. Moreover, when there are fewer cars, public transit usually improves because the city invests in buses and trains instead. Now, on the other hand, I do recognize that some drivers and small businesses worry about access, especially people who carry heavy goods or have mobility issues. That's a real concern the city must address with good transit and delivery zones. Even so, with proper planning, the long-term benefits to health, safety, and the environment are simply too important to ignore. In conclusion, I strongly support limiting cars downtown, as long as cities make sure everyone can still get where they need to go.

title: Moving Abroad For Opportunities
prompt_text: Many people choose to move to another country in search of better job prospects, education, or quality of life. Some believe the rewards are worth the sacrifice, while others feel that leaving home costs too much. In your opinion, is it worth moving to another country for better opportunities? Support your answer with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether it is worth it, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, fairly acknowledge the emotional cost, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, it is absolutely worth moving to another country for better opportunities, even though it isn't easy. To start with, the career and financial benefits can completely change a person's future. A good example is my older brother, who moved to Canada for an engineering job that simply didn't exist back home; within a few years, he was earning more and doing work he genuinely loves. Furthermore, living abroad opens your mind in ways nothing else can. You learn a new language, meet people from different backgrounds, and become far more adaptable and confident. That said, I won't pretend it's painless. Leaving family behind is hard, and culture shock and homesickness are very real, especially in the first year. I felt that loneliness myself when I first arrived. However, I truly believe these difficulties are temporary, while the long-term gains in opportunity and personal growth last a lifetime. All in all, although moving to another country requires real sacrifice and courage, the doors it opens make it more than worth it.

title: High-Rise Apartments Or Houses
prompt_text: When choosing a home, some people prefer living in a high-rise apartment, while others would rather have a house. Each option has its own advantages. Which do you think makes a better home, a high-rise apartment or a house? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose one option, give reason one with an example, add reason two with an example, briefly admit the strengths of the other choice, then restate your preference.
sample_response_band12: I would say that a house makes a better home than a high-rise apartment, mainly because of the space and freedom it gives you. For one thing, a house usually offers far more room, both inside and outside. For instance, my family has a small backyard, so my kids can play outside safely while we have barbecues with our neighbours, which would be impossible in an apartment. Another reason is privacy. In a house, you don't share walls, elevators, or hallways with dozens of strangers, so it's much quieter and you really feel like the place is your own. You can also paint the walls, plant a garden, or get a pet without asking anyone's permission. By contrast, I do see the appeal of high-rise apartments. They often come with great amenities like gyms and pools, strong security, and low maintenance. Those are genuine advantages, especially for busy young professionals. Even so, for comfort, space, and a real sense of home, I personally prefer a house. Overall, I believe a house simply offers a richer place to build family life.

title: Suburbs Versus Cities For Families
prompt_text: When deciding where to raise children, some families choose the suburbs for the space and safety, while others prefer the energy and resources of the city. Do you think the suburbs are a better place to raise a family than the city? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State which is better for families, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the merits of the other, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, the suburbs are generally a better place to raise a family than the city. First of all, suburbs tend to be safer and quieter, with less traffic and crime. For example, my sister moved to a suburb when her daughter was born, and now her kids can ride their bikes around the neighbourhood without her constantly worrying. Secondly, suburbs usually offer much more space and greenery for the same price. Families can afford a bigger home with a yard, and there are parks and playgrounds nearby where children can run around and stay active. On top of that, suburban communities are often very close, so neighbours actually know one another and look out for each other's kids. While it's true that cities offer wonderful advantages, like museums, diverse cultural experiences, and a shorter commute to work, those benefits often come with higher costs and a more stressful pace of life. For me, when children are young, space, safety, and community matter most. To conclude, I firmly believe the suburbs give families a calmer, healthier environment in which children can grow.

title: Does Tourism Help Or Harm Cities
prompt_text: Popular cities around the world attract millions of tourists every year. Some people argue that tourism brings jobs and money, while others worry it causes overcrowding and damage to local communities. In your opinion, does tourism do more good than harm to popular cities? Support your answer with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether tourism does more good than harm, give reason one with an example, add reason two with an example, fairly acknowledge the downsides, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: I strongly feel that tourism does more good than harm to popular cities, as long as it's managed well. To begin with, tourism creates a huge number of jobs and brings money into the local economy. A clear example is my hometown, where hotels, restaurants, and tour companies employ thousands of people, and many small family businesses survive entirely because of visitors. What's more, tourism encourages cultural exchange and helps preserve heritage. When people travel, they learn about other ways of life, and the money they spend often funds the restoration of historic buildings and museums that might otherwise fall apart. Admittedly, tourism does have a darker side. Too many visitors can cause overcrowding, push up housing prices for locals, and put real strain on roads and public services. I've seen famous neighbourhoods become so packed that residents can barely enjoy their own streets. However, these problems usually come from poor planning, not from tourism itself, and they can be controlled with smart rules and limits. In the end, I believe the economic and cultural benefits clearly outweigh the drawbacks, so tourism does far more good than harm overall.

title: Dual-Income Or Stay-At-Home Parent
prompt_text: Many families today face a choice about how to balance work and raising children.
