Some people believe it is better for both parents to have jobs, while others think one parent should stay home to care for the family. In your opinion, which arrangement is better for a family? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State which arrangement you prefer, give your first reason with a concrete example, add a second reason with another example, briefly acknowledge the benefits of the other choice, then restate your position firmly.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, it's generally better for both parents to work, although I completely understand why some families choose otherwise. First of all, having two incomes gives a family real financial stability. For instance, my own parents both worked, and that's the only reason we could afford things like music lessons and a family vacation every summer. Secondly, when both parents are employed, children actually see two strong role models who are independent and engaged with the world. My mother always told me her job made her a happier, more patient person at home. Now, I do recognize the other side. A stay-at-home parent can offer constant attention and a calm, structured routine, which is especially valuable when kids are very young. That's a genuine benefit I wouldn't dismiss. However, with good childcare and shared housework, a dual-income household can give children both security and great role models. So, all things considered, I firmly believe that having both parents work tends to be the stronger choice for most modern families, as long as they make time to be present for their kids.

title: Siblings Versus Being An Only Child
prompt_text: People often have different views about the ideal family size for children. Some believe it is better to grow up with siblings, while others think being an only child has more advantages. Which do you think is better, having brothers and sisters or being an only child? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Clearly choose one side, support it with a first reason and personal example, add a second reason with an example, acknowledge one strength of the other option, and close by restating your choice.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe it's better to grow up with siblings, even though being an only child certainly has its perks. To begin with, siblings give you built-in companionship. For example, my older brother and I spent countless rainy afternoons inventing games together, and honestly, I never once felt lonely as a kid. In addition, having brothers and sisters teaches you how to share, compromise, and resolve conflicts early in life. I had to learn to negotiate over the TV remote, and that skill helps me get along with coworkers today. That said, I do see the appeal of being an only child. You get your parents' undivided attention and often more resources, which can be a real advantage. Some only children I know are remarkably confident and self-reliant because of it. Still, for me, the relationships you build with siblings are something special that lasts a lifetime. So in conclusion, while both situations have their benefits, I would always choose growing up with siblings, because that lifelong bond and those early social lessons are simply priceless.

title: Using Apps To Make Friends
prompt_text: These days, more and more people use mobile apps to meet new people and make friends. Some people think these apps are a convenient and effective way to build a social circle, while others believe meeting people in person is far better. Do you think apps are a good way to make new friends? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you think apps are good for making friends, give one reason with an example, give a second reason with an example, fairly acknowledge a drawback, then restate your overall position.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think apps can be a good way to make new friends, as long as people use them sensibly. First and foremost, they're incredibly convenient and they widen your pool of potential connections. For instance, a friend of mine moved to a new city where she knew nobody, and an app let her meet people who shared her love of hiking and live music. Another point is that you can learn a bit about someone before you ever meet, which helps you find common ground quickly. My cousin actually found her closest group of friends this way, and they bonded over a shared interest in cooking. However, I won't pretend apps are perfect. People sometimes misrepresent themselves, and chatting on a screen isn't the same as a real first impression, so you do have to stay cautious and prioritize online safety. Even so, the same risks exist when you meet someone at a party or a club. To sum up, I believe apps are a genuinely useful tool for building a social circle today, provided you stay honest, careful, and open to meeting people face to face soon.

title: Can Long-Distance Relationships Succeed
prompt_text: Many couples today end up living far apart because of work, study, or family reasons. Some people believe long-distance relationships can be just as successful as ordinary ones, while others think the distance usually causes them to fail. In your opinion, can long-distance relationships succeed? Explain your view with reasons and examples.
vocabulary_tips: absence makes the heart grow fonder
template_hint: Take a clear position on whether they can succeed, support it with a main reason and example, add a second reason with an example, acknowledge the opposing concern, and conclude by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: In my view, long-distance relationships absolutely can succeed, though they definitely require extra effort. The main reason is that with today's technology, staying close has never been easier. For example, my sister dated someone overseas for two years, and they video-called every single night, which kept them more connected than some couples who live in the same town. Furthermore, distance often forces couples to communicate more honestly and to truly commit. When you can't rely on just being physically together, you have to talk through your feelings, and that builds a really strong foundation of trust. On the other hand, I do understand the worry that couples can slowly drift apart, or that loneliness and jealousy can creep in over time. That's a real challenge, and not every relationship survives it. However, the same problems can sink couples who live next door to each other. So, all in all, I firmly believe that with strong communication, trust, and a clear plan to eventually be together, long-distance relationships can be every bit as successful as any other kind.

title: Quality Family Time These Days
prompt_text: Some people feel that modern families no longer spend enough meaningful time together. Others argue that families today still connect plenty, just in newer ways. Do you think families nowadays spend enough quality time together? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you think families spend enough quality time, give one reason with an example, add a second reason with an example, concede a valid point from the other side, then restate your conclusion.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I believe most families today do not spend enough quality time together, although there are certainly exceptions. One reason for this is that everyone is simply overwhelmed by busy schedules. Take, for example, a typical family I know: the parents work long hours, the kids have homework and activities, and by dinnertime everyone is exhausted and just wants to be alone. On top of that, screens have quietly stolen our attention. Even when families are in the same room, they're often scrolling on separate phones instead of actually talking, so they're physically together but emotionally apart. I see this constantly in restaurants, where a whole family sits silently staring at their devices. Admittedly, some families do make a real effort, planning game nights or weekend trips, and technology can even help relatives who live far away stay in touch. That's a fair point. Still, those families feel like the minority to me. To wrap up, while I deeply admire families who protect their time together, I think the general trend is that modern life pulls us apart, and most of us need to be far more intentional about connecting.

title: Are Pets Family Members
prompt_text: Pets play an important role in many people's lives. Some people believe pets should be treated as real members of the family, while others think they are simply animals that people keep. In your opinion, should pets be considered members of the family? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State your position on whether pets are family, give a first reason with an example, give a second reason with an example, acknowledge a reasonable objection, and finish by restating your view.
sample_response_band12: I strongly feel that pets should absolutely be considered members of the family. To start with, pets give us genuine companionship and unconditional love, just like the people we live with. For instance, my dog greets me at the door every single day, and after a hard day at work, that loyalty honestly means the world to me. What's more, caring for a pet involves the same kind of commitment and responsibility you'd give any family member. We make sure our cat sees the vet, eats well, and feels safe, and we'd never abandon her when things got difficult. She's part of our daily routine and our hearts. Of course, I understand that some people feel a pet is still an animal and shouldn't be put on the same level as a child or a parent. That's a fair distinction, and I'm not saying they're identical. Still, the emotional bond we share with our pets is deep and very real. In short, while pets may differ from human relatives, the love, loyalty, and care that flow both ways make them, in my eyes, true members of the family.

title: Should Grandparents Help Raise Grandchildren
prompt_text: In many families, grandparents play an active role in looking after their grandchildren. Some people believe grandparents should help raise the children, while others think parents should handle this responsibility on their own. In your opinion, should grandparents help raise their grandchildren? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether grandparents should help, support it with a first reason and example, add a second reason with an example, fairly acknowledge a downside, then restate your conclusion.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, grandparents should definitely help raise their grandchildren, as long as it's done thoughtfully. The first reason is that grandparents bring decades of wisdom and experience that parents simply don't have yet. To give an example, when I was little, my grandmother taught me patience and passed on family traditions my busy parents had no time for. Beyond that, grandparents provide a vital support system, especially for working parents who struggle to afford childcare. My aunt, a single mom, could only keep her job because my grandfather watched the kids after school every day, which kept the whole family afloat. Granted, I do recognize the risks. Differing parenting styles can cause friction, and it isn't fair to treat grandparents as unpaid babysitters when they deserve to enjoy their retirement. Those concerns are completely valid. However, with clear communication and mutual respect, most of those tensions can be managed. Ultimately, I firmly believe that when grandparents are willing and able, their involvement enriches children's lives enormously and strengthens the entire family across generations.

title: Watching Films At Home
prompt_text: Some people prefer watching films at home, where they feel comfortable and can pause whenever they like. Others believe going to the cinema gives a much better experience with a big screen and powerful sound. Which do you think is better, watching films at home or going to the cinema? Give reasons and examples to explain your opinion.
template_hint: State which option you prefer, give two reasons each backed by a concrete example, briefly acknowledge the appeal of the other option, then restate your position with a strong closing line.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, watching films at home is definitely the better option, mainly because of comfort and convenience. First of all, when I watch a movie at home, I can relax on my own couch, pause whenever I need a snack, and even rewind a scene I missed. For instance, last weekend I watched a thriller and paused it twice to make tea without missing a single line. What's more, it's far more cost-effective. A cinema ticket plus popcorn can easily cost twenty or thirty dollars, whereas a streaming subscription gives me hundreds of films for the price of one ticket. On the other hand, I do admit the cinema offers an immersive experience with a huge screen and powerful sound, which is hard to match at home. However, modern televisions and sound systems have improved so much that the gap is really shrinking. All things considered, the flexibility, the savings, and the freedom from crowds make watching films at home my clear preference, and that's how I enjoy movies almost every week.

title: Book Versus Film Adaptation
prompt_text: Many people argue that reading a book is more rewarding than watching the film based on it, because the reader can imagine the story for themselves. Others say the film version is more enjoyable and easier to follow. Do you think reading a book is better than watching its film adaptation? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Clearly state whether you favour the book or the film, support it with two reasons and specific examples, concede one strength of the other format, then restate your overall judgement.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that reading a book is generally better than watching its film adaptation. To begin with, a novel lets you use your own imagination to picture the characters and settings exactly as you want. For example, when I read a fantasy series, I created my own vision of the kingdom in my head, but the movie's version looked completely different and honestly disappointed me. Furthermore, books offer far more depth. Authors can explore a character's inner thoughts and motivations in detail, while a two-hour film simply has to leave a lot of that out. When I watched the film of my favourite mystery, several important subplots were missing, so the ending felt rushed and confusing. On the other hand, I'll admit that films have one clear advantage: they bring stories to life visually with stunning effects and music, which can be very powerful. However, that visual spectacle rarely makes up for the lost richness of the original text. To sum up, because reading sparks imagination and provides far greater depth, I'm convinced the book is almost always the better choice.

title: News From Social Media
prompt_text: These days, many people get their news mainly from social media platforms instead of newspapers or television. Some say this is a fast and convenient way to stay informed, while others worry that the information is often inaccurate or misleading. In your opinion, is getting news from social media reliable? Give reasons and examples to explain your view.
template_hint: Take a clear stance on reliability, give two reasons with real examples, acknowledge a benefit or risk of the opposing view, then conclude by reaffirming your position.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think getting news mainly from social media is not very reliable, even though it is undeniably fast. First and foremost, anyone can post anything online, so false stories spread quickly before they are ever fact-checked. A good example of this is during emergencies, when rumours about disasters or health scares go viral within minutes and cause real panic, only to be proven wrong later. Moreover, social media platforms use algorithms that show us content based on what we already like, so we end up trapped in a bubble that reinforces our opinions rather than informing us with balanced facts. However, I do recognise one genuine advantage: social media can deliver breaking news instantly and even let ordinary people share events the mainstream media miss. The problem is that speed without verification is dangerous. That's why I always cross-check anything important against a credible newspaper or an established broadcaster before believing it. In conclusion, while social media is a useful starting point, it simply isn't trustworthy enough on its own, and relying on it as your only news source is a mistake.

title: Celebrity Influence On Youth
prompt_text: Some people feel that celebrities have far too much influence on young people, shaping how they dress, behave, and even what they value. Others believe celebrities can be positive role models who inspire young people to achieve their goals. Do you think celebrities have too much influence on young people? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you think the influence is excessive, give two supporting reasons with examples, briefly recognise the opposite viewpoint, then restate your conclusion firmly.
sample_response_band12: I would argue that celebrities do have too much influence on young people, and this can be quite harmful. One reason is that teenagers are very impressionable and tend to copy whatever their idols do. Take, for example, the way social media stars promote certain looks; many young people develop unrealistic standards about their bodies and feel insecure when they can't match those edited, perfect images. In addition, celebrities often advertise expensive products, which creates real pressure on teens to buy things they cannot afford just to fit in with their friends. I've seen classmates beg their parents for designer items simply because a famous singer was wearing them. That said, I do acknowledge that some celebrities are excellent role models who use their platform to encourage education, kindness, or charity work, and that influence is genuinely positive. However, those good examples are outnumbered by the constant pressure to look and live a certain way. Overall, because young people are so easily swayed and exposed to unrealistic ideals, I firmly believe celebrities hold more influence over them than is healthy.

title: Are Video Games Beneficial
prompt_text: Some people believe that video games are a waste of time and can be bad for your health. Others argue that video games can be beneficial, helping people develop skills and connect with others. In your opinion, can video games be beneficial? Give reasons and examples to explain your view.
template_hint: State your position on whether games are beneficial, support it with two reasons and examples, acknowledge a downside raised by the opposing side, then close by reaffirming your view.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that video games can be beneficial, as long as they are played in moderation. To start with, many games sharpen valuable mental skills such as problem-solving and quick decision-making. For instance, strategy games require players to plan several steps ahead and manage limited resources, which is excellent training for the brain. My younger cousin actually improved his math and planning skills after playing these kinds of games regularly. Besides that, video games are a wonderful way to connect with others. Online multiplayer games let people team up with friends and even make new ones around the world, which is especially valuable for people who feel lonely or isolated. On the other hand, I do admit that too much gaming can harm your health and reduce time for studying or exercise, so balance is essential. However, that is a problem of overuse, not of the games themselves. In short, when played sensibly, video games build real skills and bring people together, so I am convinced they can be genuinely beneficial.

title: Streaming and Excessive Watching
prompt_text: With the rise of streaming services, people now have instant access to thousands of shows and movies at any time. Some people think this has caused many of us to watch far too much and waste valuable time. Others say streaming simply gives people more choice and harmless entertainment. Do you think streaming services have made people watch too much? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether streaming has led to excessive watching, give two reasons with examples, concede a point from the other side, then restate your overall opinion.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, streaming services have definitely encouraged people to watch far too much. The main reason is that these platforms are deliberately designed to keep us hooked. Features like autoplay automatically start the next episode within seconds, so before you know it, you've watched an entire season in one sitting. A clear example is binge-watching; I've stayed up until two in the morning finishing a series simply because I never had to make the effort to press play again. On top of that, the sheer amount of endless content removes the natural stopping points we used to have. In the past, a TV show aired once a week, so you had to wait, but now thousands of titles are available instantly, which makes it very hard to practise self-control. Admittedly, streaming does offer wonderful variety and convenience, and for people with genuine time management skills it can be perfectly harmless entertainment. However, most of us struggle to resist that constant temptation. To conclude, because these services are engineered to maximise our viewing time, I firmly believe they have caused people to watch much more than is good for them.

title: Advertising Aimed At Children
prompt_text: Children are exposed to a great deal of advertising on television, apps, and websites every day. Some people argue that advertising aimed at children should be restricted because young children cannot tell the difference between ads and real content. Others believe such restrictions are unnecessary and that parents should simply guide their children. In your opinion, should advertising aimed at children be restricted? Give reasons and examples to explain your view.
template_hint: State whether such advertising should be restricted, give two reasons with concrete examples, acknowledge the counterargument, then reaffirm your position.
sample_response_band12: In my view, advertising aimed at children should definitely be restricted, because young children are simply too vulnerable to protect themselves. First of all, small children cannot tell the difference between an advertisement and ordinary programming, so they end up believing whatever they are told. A good illustration is the way cartoons are packed with ads for sugary cereals and junk food; children pester their parents to buy these products, and this constant pressure contributes to poor diets and even childhood obesity. Furthermore, this kind of advertising encourages harmful consumerism from a very early age, teaching kids that happiness comes from owning the newest toy. I recently watched my little nephew throw a tantrum simply because he saw a flashy toy advert and felt he had to have it. Some might argue that restrictions are unnecessary and that parents should take responsibility for what their children watch. While guidance certainly matters, parents cannot possibly monitor every single screen their child sees all day. All in all, because children are so easily influenced and the consequences can affect their health, I strongly believe such advertising must be properly regulated.

title: Required Community Service For Students
prompt_text: Some schools believe that students should be required to complete a certain number of community service hours in order to graduate. Others feel that helping the community should be a personal choice, not a requirement. Do you think community service should be mandatory for students? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason one with a concrete example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your opinion in a strong closing sentence.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, community service should definitely be required for students. First of all, it teaches young people about civic responsibility at an age when habits are still forming. For instance, when I was in high school, our school made us volunteer forty hours, and I ended up helping at a food bank every Saturday. That experience completely changed how I saw my own community. What's more, community service builds practical skills you simply can't learn in a classroom, like teamwork, communication, and time management. A friend of mine discovered her passion for nursing only because she was required to volunteer at a hospital. On the other hand, some people argue that forcing students removes the genuine motivation behind helping others, and I understand that concern. However, I'd say that even if students start because they have to, many continue because they want to once they see the impact. All things considered, making community service mandatory introduces young people to a habit of giving back that often lasts a lifetime, so I firmly believe it's worth requiring.

title: Should Voting Be Mandatory
prompt_text: In some countries, voting in elections is required for all adult citizens, while in others it is completely optional. Some people believe everyone should be required to vote, while others think people should be free to decide for themselves. Do you think voting should be mandatory for all adults? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Take a clear side, support it with two reasons backed by examples, acknowledge the freedom-based counterargument, and finish by reaffirming your position.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I believe voting should be mandatory for all adults. To begin with, when everyone is required to vote, the results truly represent the whole population rather than just the people who feel strongly about an issue. For example, in countries where voting is compulsory, turnout is often above ninety percent, which means leaders are chosen by the majority, not by a small, motivated group. In addition, requiring people to vote encourages them to become more engaged citizens. If I knew I had to vote, I'd naturally pay more attention to the issues and make a more informed decision. I've actually seen this with my own cousin, who only started following the news once she realized her vote really mattered. However, I do understand the argument that voting is a personal freedom and that forcing people might lead to careless choices. Even so, I think the benefit of having a government that reflects everyone outweighs that concern, especially since people can always submit a blank ballot. To sum up, mandatory voting strengthens our communities by making sure every voice is counted.

title: Are Public Libraries Still Important
prompt_text: With so much information now available online, some people believe that public libraries are no longer necessary. Others argue that libraries still play an important role in their communities. Do you think public libraries are still important today? Give reasons and examples to support your view.
template_hint: Declare your opinion, present two supporting reasons each with a real example, address the it's-all-online counterpoint, and close with a confident restatement.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that public libraries are still incredibly important today. First and foremost, libraries provide free access to resources that not everyone can afford. Take, for example, students from low-income families who don't have computers or fast internet at home. The library is often the only place where they can do research or apply for jobs online, which really helps bridge the digital divide. Beyond that, libraries have become true community hubs, not just rows of books. In my neighbourhood, the local library hosts free reading programs for kids, language classes for newcomers, and even job-search workshops. It's a quiet, welcoming space where anyone can spend an afternoon without spending money. Some might say that everything is online now, so we don't need physical libraries anymore. While the internet is convenient, it isn't free for everyone, and it can't replace the human connection and guidance that librarians offer. In conclusion, public libraries do far more than lend books; they support learning, equality, and community, which is exactly why I believe they remain essential today.

title: Individual Success Versus Community
prompt_text: Some people think that individuals today focus too much on their own personal success, while others believe that ambition and achievement benefit everyone. Do you think people today place too much emphasis on individual success rather than the good of the community? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Stake out your position, support it with two reasons and examples, fairly acknowledge the opposing perspective, then deliver a clear final judgment.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, people today do focus too much on individual success, sometimes at the expense of the community. On one hand, our culture constantly celebrates personal achievement, whether it's career titles, money, or social media followers. A good example of this is how many young professionals work extremely long hours chasing promotions while barely knowing their own neighbours. That competitive mindset can leave people feeling isolated and disconnected. Furthermore, when everyone is purely focused on themselves, important community work often gets ignored. In my own building, almost no one volunteers for the residents' committee anymore because people simply don't have the time or interest. On the other hand, I do recognize that ambition isn't a bad thing; individual success can create jobs, inspire others, and even fund charities. That being said, I think we've tipped too far toward the individual and lost some of our sense of belonging. Ultimately, I believe a healthy society needs both personal drive and genuine care for the collective wellbeing, and right now we're leaning a little too heavily on the former.

title: Donating Money Or Volunteering Time
prompt_text: When people want to support a cause, some choose to donate money, while others prefer to volunteer their time. Which do you think helps a cause more, donating money or volunteering time? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose one side clearly, justify it with two reasons and examples, concede a genuine strength of the other option, and end by reasserting your choice.
sample_response_band12: If you ask me, volunteering your time usually helps a cause more than simply donating money. The main reason is that volunteers provide hands-on help that money alone can't always buy. For instance, when there was a flood in a nearby town, what people needed most wasn't cash; it was actual hands to fill sandbags, cook meals, and clean up homes. That kind of personal involvement creates a tangible impact you can see immediately. Another point worth mentioning is that volunteering builds long-term connections between people and the cause. When I spent a few weekends at an animal shelter, I became so invested that I kept coming back and even brought friends along. Admittedly, money is extremely important too, because organizations need funds to pay for supplies, rent, and staff, and not everyone has free time to give. Without donations, many charities couldn't operate at all. Still, money can sometimes be spent inefficiently, whereas a dedicated volunteer's effort goes directly where it's needed. All in all, while both clearly matter, I believe giving your time creates a deeper and more lasting difference.

title: Less Connected To Local Communities
prompt_text: Some people feel that, compared to the past, individuals today are less connected to their local communities. Others believe that people are just as connected as before, only in different ways. Do you think people today are less connected to their neighbourhoods than they used to be? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Present your stance, back it with two reasons and concrete examples, acknowledge the alternative interpretation, and wrap up with a decisive closing line.
sample_response_band12: I tend to think that people today are indeed less connected to their local communities than they used to be. To start with, our fast-paced lifestyle leaves very little time for neighbourly interaction. A clear example is my own parents, who told me that years ago everyone on their street knew each other and shared meals, whereas today I barely recognize the people living next door to me. On top of that, technology, while wonderful, has shifted our attention online. Instead of chatting at the local park or shop, many of us scroll through our phones and talk to friends who live far away, which often comes at the cost of real, face-to-face connection in our own neighbourhoods. Having said that, I do see the argument that people are simply connected differently now, through community apps and online groups, and those tools can genuinely help. Even so, I don't think a message in a group chat fully replaces actually knowing your neighbours in person. In the end, I believe we've gained global connections but lost some of that close-knit, local sense of community we once had.

title: Shared Responsibility For Clean Spaces
prompt_text: Some people believe that keeping public spaces such as parks and streets clean is the shared responsibility of everyone who uses them. Others feel it is mainly the job of the government and cleaning workers. Do you think keeping public spaces clean is everyone's shared responsibility? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Assert your opinion, support it with two reasons and examples, fairly note the opposing view about authorities, then close with a firm restatement.
sample_response_band12: I am convinced that keeping public spaces clean is absolutely everyone's shared responsibility. First off, public parks and streets belong to all of us, so it only makes sense that each person should take ownership of them. To illustrate, if everyone simply threw their wrappers on the ground assuming someone else would clean up, our parks would be unusable within days. I've actually seen a beautiful local beach turn into a mess after a busy weekend, and that wasn't the government's fault; it was the visitors'. Not only that, but when people see others respecting a space, they tend to follow suit. Cleaning up after yourself, or even picking up a stray bottle, sets a positive example that spreads through a community. Granted, the government and cleaning workers do play an essential role, and we definitely need them to provide bins and maintain large areas. However, no city can hire enough staff to follow every single person around. To conclude, while authorities certainly help, keeping our shared spaces clean ultimately depends on a collective effort, and I firmly believe everyone has a part to play.

title: Travelling Alone Or Together
prompt_text: Some people prefer to travel alone, enjoying the freedom to do whatever they want. Others believe that travelling with friends or family is more enjoyable and safer. Which do you think is better, travelling alone or travelling with others? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason 1 with a concrete example, give reason 2 with an example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your opinion in a strong final sentence.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, travelling with others is definitely better than travelling alone, and I feel strongly about this for a couple of reasons. First of all, sharing the experience makes it far more memorable. For instance, last year I went to Banff with two close friends, and being able to laugh about getting lost on a hiking trail turned a stressful moment into a story we still tell today. You simply can't share that kind of inside joke when you're on your own. Secondly, travelling together is much safer and more practical. When one person is exhausted or feeling unwell, someone else can step in, navigate, or watch the bags. That peace of mind really matters in an unfamiliar city. Now, I do understand why some people love solo travel, because it gives you complete freedom and flexibility over your schedule. However, for me, the joy of companionship easily outweighs that independence. So, all things considered, while travelling alone has its appeal, I genuinely believe that exploring the world alongside people you care about makes every trip richer and more rewarding.

title: Does Travel Broaden The Mind
prompt_text: Some people say that travelling truly broadens a person's mind by exposing them to new cultures and ideas. Others argue that you can become open-minded without ever leaving home. Do you agree or disagree that travelling broadens the mind? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Take a clear side, support it with reason 1 and a personal or concrete example, add reason 2 with an example, concede the other viewpoint, and close by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that travelling truly does broaden a person's mind, and I'd like to explain why. To begin with, travelling forces you to step outside your comfort zone and challenge your preconceptions. For example, before I visited a small town in Mexico, I assumed life there would be quite difficult, but I came away deeply impressed by how warm and generous the community was. That firsthand experience completely reshaped my perspective in a way no documentary ever could. What's more, travel builds genuine cultural awareness. When you actually taste the food, hear the language, and navigate unfamiliar customs, you start to appreciate that there are many valid ways of living. I now find myself far more patient and curious about people who are different from me. In contrast, some argue you can become open-minded through books and the internet, and there is some truth to that. However, reading about a place is simply not the same as standing in it. In conclusion, while learning from home certainly helps, I'm convinced that nothing expands your worldview quite like experiencing other cultures in person.

title: Learning The Local Language
prompt_text: Some people think that tourists should learn at least a few words of the local language before visiting a country. Others feel this is unnecessary because English is spoken in many places. Do you agree or disagree that tourists should learn some of the local language? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your opinion, explain reason 1 with an example, explain reason 2 with an example, acknowledge the counterargument, and finish with a confident summary of your view.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think tourists absolutely should learn at least a few words of the local language before visiting a country. First and foremost, making that small effort shows respect and goes a long way with the people you meet. To give an example, when I visited Italy, I only knew how to say hello, please, and thank you, but the shopkeepers immediately warmed up to me and even gave me tips on the best places to eat. That simple courtesy completely changed how I was treated. Secondly, knowing a few basic phrases makes travel far smoother and safer. Being able to ask for directions or order food helps you avoid confusion and feel more confident in unfamiliar surroundings. Of course, I understand the argument that English is widely spoken, so learning the language may seem unnecessary. Even so, relying entirely on English can come across as a little lazy or even disrespectful. To sum up, while you certainly don't need to be fluent, I genuinely believe that learning a handful of local words enriches your trip and helps you connect with the people there.

title: Taking A Gap Year To Travel
prompt_text: Some young people choose to take a gap year to travel before starting university or a career. Some believe this is a valuable life experience, while others think it wastes time and delays important goals. Do you think taking a gap year to travel is a good idea? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Declare your position, develop reason 1 with an example, add reason 2 with an example, fairly acknowledge the opposing concern, and end by restating why your view holds.
sample_response_band12: I'm strongly in favour of taking a gap year to travel, and I think it can be one of the best decisions a young person makes. One major reason is that travelling builds maturity and real-world skills you simply can't learn in a classroom. Take, for instance, a friend of mine who spent a year backpacking through Asia. She came back far more confident, independent, and capable of solving problems on her own, which actually helped her thrive at university. On top of that, a gap year gives you time for self-discovery before committing to a career path. By stepping away from constant pressure, many people return with a much clearer sense of what they truly want to study or do. Admittedly, some worry that taking a year off delays important goals and breaks academic momentum, and I can see why parents might be nervous. However, a single year is a tiny investment compared to a lifetime of work. All in all, when it's planned thoughtfully, I firmly believe a gap year broadens your horizons and prepares you for the future rather than holding you back.

title: Budget Travel Versus Luxury Travel
prompt_text: Some travellers prefer budget travel, such as staying in hostels and using public transport, because they feel it is more authentic. Others enjoy luxury travel for its comfort and convenience. Which do you think is more rewarding, budget travel or luxury travel? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
connector_phrases: When all is said and done
template_hint: Choose your side, justify it with reason 1 and an example, give reason 2 with an example, recognise the merits of the other option, then reassert your preference.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, budget travel is far more rewarding than luxury travel, even though it may sound less comfortable. The main reason is that travelling on a budget pushes you into more authentic experiences. As an illustration, when I stayed in a small family-run hostel in Portugal, the owners invited me to a neighbourhood dinner, and I ended up learning to cook a traditional dish. You would never get that kind of genuine connection inside a polished five-star resort. Furthermore, budget travel forces you off the beaten path and teaches you to be resourceful. Taking local buses and chatting with people at markets gave me a much deeper sense of the culture, and those small adventures are the memories that stuck with me. Granted, I understand the appeal of luxury travel; sometimes it's wonderful to splurge and let someone else handle every detail. However, that comfort often keeps you in a bubble, separated from real life. When all is said and done, I believe the richest travel memories come not from luxury, but from the unexpected moments that budget travel makes possible.

title: Photographing Trips Or Enjoying Them
prompt_text: Some people believe that travellers today focus too much on taking photos and posting on social media instead of actually enjoying their trips. Others argue that photos help capture and share precious memories. Do you agree or disagree that people focus too much on photographing trips rather than experiencing them? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your opinion firmly, back it with reason 1 and an example, add reason 2 with an example, give credit to the opposing argument, and conclude by reinforcing your position.
sample_response_band12: To be honest, I believe many travellers today really do focus too much on photographing their trips instead of genuinely enjoying them. First off, constantly chasing the perfect photo becomes a major distraction that pulls you out of the moment. A good example of this is something I noticed at a beautiful waterfall in Iceland, where dozens of people were so busy filming for social media that they barely looked at the view with their own eyes. They were experiencing the whole thing behind a screen. More importantly, this obsession can turn a relaxing holiday into stressful work, as people worry about likes and comments rather than simply unwinding. I've felt that pressure myself, and it honestly ruined part of a trip. Having said that, I do recognise the other side, because photos are a wonderful way to preserve memories and share special moments with family who couldn't be there. The key, I think, is to strike a balance. In the end, while taking a few pictures is perfectly fine, I'm convinced that too many travellers sacrifice real experiences for the sake of their cameras.

title: Limiting Tourists At Popular Destinations
prompt_text: Some popular destinations have become extremely crowded, and many people argue that these places should limit the number of tourists allowed to visit. Others feel that everyone should have the right to travel wherever they wish. Do you agree or disagree that popular destinations should limit the number of tourists? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Take a clear stance, support it with reason 1 and an example, add reason 2 with an example, fairly address the counterargument, and finish by restating your conclusion.
sample_response_band12: I would argue that popular destinations absolutely should limit the number of tourists they allow, even though it may seem unfair at first. The first point is that overcrowding causes serious environmental damage to fragile places. Consider, for example, Venice, where massive crowds and cruise ships have worn down historic sites and polluted the canals. Without limits, these treasures could be damaged beyond repair, robbing future generations of the chance to see them. On top of that, excessive tourism makes life miserable for local residents. In cities like Barcelona, locals have been priced out of their own neighbourhoods and can barely move through streets packed with visitors, which is simply not sustainable. While it's true that travel should be open to everyone, and I understand that argument, freedom to travel shouldn't come at the cost of destroying a place entirely. A reasonable cap, perhaps through a booking system, actually protects the experience for everyone. Ultimately, I'm convinced that thoughtfully limiting tourist numbers is the responsible choice, because preserving these incredible destinations matters far more than allowing unlimited access today.

title: Cooking At Home Versus Eating Out
prompt_text: Some people believe that cooking meals at home is better than eating out at restaurants. Others think that eating out is more convenient and enjoyable. Which option do you think is better? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State which option you prefer, give one reason with a concrete example, give a second reason with an example, briefly admit the appeal of the other choice, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, cooking at home is definitely the better choice for most people, and I'd like to explain why. First of all, it's far more cost-effective. For instance, when I make a big pot of pasta at home, it costs me maybe four dollars and feeds me for two days, whereas the same dish at a restaurant would easily set me back fifteen. Secondly, cooking at home gives you complete control over what goes into your food. You can cut back on salt, use fresh vegetables, and adjust portions to suit your needs, which is much healthier in the long run. What's more, I find cooking genuinely relaxing after a long day. Now, I'll admit that eating out is wonderfully convenient, especially when you're exhausted or celebrating something special. There's no denying the joy of a meal you didn't prepare. However, those occasions should be treats rather than the norm. All things considered, I firmly believe that cooking at home wins out because it saves money, keeps you healthy, and can be a rewarding part of your daily routine.

title: Is Organic Food Worth The Price
prompt_text: Organic food is usually more expensive than regular food. Some people think the higher price is worth it for the health and environmental benefits, while others believe it is simply not worth paying extra. What is your opinion? Give reasons and examples to explain your answer.
template_hint: Take a clear stance on whether organic food is worth the price, support it with two reasons and examples, acknowledge the opposing concern, and finish by reaffirming your view.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I don't think organic food is always worth its higher price, though I do understand the appeal. To begin with, the price difference can be enormous. For example, organic strawberries at my local store cost almost double what the regular ones do, and for a family on a tight budget, that's a significant burden every single week. More importantly, the scientific evidence on whether organic food is actually more nutritious is far from conclusive. A lot of the premium price, in my view, comes down to marketing hype rather than proven benefits. That being said, I do recognize that organic farming is generally more sustainable and avoids heavy pesticide use, which is genuinely better for the environment and for farm workers, so I'm not dismissing it entirely. For certain items, like the so-called dirty dozen, paying extra might make sense. In conclusion, while organic food does have real environmental advantages, I firmly believe that for most everyday shoppers it simply isn't worth the steep premium. I'd rather spend my money on a wider variety of fresh produce than pay double just for a label.

title: Is A Vegetarian Diet Better
prompt_text: Some people argue that a vegetarian diet is healthier and better for the planet, so most people should follow it. Others believe that eating meat is a natural and important part of a balanced diet. Do you agree or disagree that a vegetarian diet is better for most people? Give reasons and examples.
template_hint: Declare whether you agree a vegetarian diet is better, give two supporting reasons with examples, fairly note the other perspective, and close with a firm restatement.
sample_response_band12: I tend to disagree with the idea that a vegetarian diet is automatically better for most people, although I respect those who choose it. On the one hand, I completely accept that eating less meat can reduce your carbon footprint and that too much red meat isn't good for anyone. Furthermore, vegetarian diets are often rich in fibre and vegetables, which is clearly beneficial. However, I believe the key word here is balance, not elimination. A good example of this is my grandfather, who ate moderate amounts of fish and chicken his whole life and stayed remarkably healthy well into his eighties. Meat is also a convenient, complete protein source, which matters a great deal for growing children, athletes, and the elderly. Cutting it out entirely requires careful planning to avoid deficiencies in iron or vitamin B12, and not everyone has the knowledge or budget to do that properly. To sum up, while I genuinely support eating more plant-based meals, I don't think a strictly vegetarian diet is the best fit for most people. In my view, a balanced diet that includes moderate amounts of meat is the healthier and more practical choice.

title: Is Breakfast The Most Important Meal
prompt_text: People often say that breakfast is the most important meal of the day. However, some people believe this is a myth and that skipping breakfast is perfectly fine. Do you agree or disagree that breakfast is the most important meal of the day? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you agree breakfast is the most important meal, give two reasons with examples, acknowledge the skipping-breakfast view, and end with a clear conclusion.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that breakfast really is one of the most important meals of the day, especially for people who are active in the mornings. The main reason is that breakfast provides the fuel your brain and body need to function well. Take, for example, students and office workers; if they skip breakfast, they often struggle to concentrate by mid-morning and end up feeling sluggish and irritable. On a busy morning without food, energy levels tend to crash around ten o'clock, and focus suffers. In addition, eating a balanced breakfast helps you avoid overeating later, because you're not starving by lunchtime and reaching for junk food. Now, some might argue that skipping breakfast is fine, and I'll admit that not everyone feels hungry first thing in the morning, so forcing food down isn't ideal either. People's bodies are genuinely different. Overall, though, I firmly believe that for most people a decent breakfast sets a positive tone for the whole day, sharpening your focus and keeping your energy steady. That's why I'd never recommend regularly skipping it.

title: Are Food-Delivery Apps A Positive Development
prompt_text: In recent years, food-delivery apps have become extremely popular. Some people see them as a positive and convenient development, while others worry about their effect on health, spending, and local restaurants. Do you think food-delivery apps are a positive development? Give reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether the apps are positive overall, give two reasons with examples, fairly mention the downside, and conclude with your overall judgment.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, food-delivery apps are largely a positive development, though they're definitely a double-edged sword. For one thing, the convenience they offer is hard to overstate. A clear example is when you're sick, swamped with work, or stuck at home in bad weather. With just a few taps, a hot meal arrives at your door, which is genuinely a lifesaver. On top of that, these apps have opened up new income opportunities for delivery drivers in the gig economy and helped small restaurants reach customers they'd never have found otherwise. During the pandemic, many local eateries survived precisely because of these platforms. Having said that, I won't pretend there are no downsides. The delivery fees add up quickly, the apps encourage impulse spending, and relying on them too often can lead to less healthy eating habits. They can also take a big cut from struggling restaurants. All in all, though, I firmly believe the benefits outweigh the drawbacks. As long as people use these apps sensibly rather than every single night, I'd say food-delivery apps are a convenient and welcome addition to modern life.

title: Should People Try Unfamiliar Cuisines
prompt_text: Some people love trying food from cultures they are unfamiliar with, while others prefer to stick to the dishes they already know and enjoy. Do you think people should try unfamiliar cuisines more often? Give reasons and examples to explain your opinion.
vocabulary_tips: step out of your comfort zone
template_hint: State whether people should try unfamiliar cuisines, support it with two reasons and examples, briefly acknowledge those who prefer the familiar, and restate your stance.
sample_response_band12: I genuinely believe that people should try unfamiliar cuisines far more often than they do. First and foremost, exploring new food is one of the easiest ways to broaden your horizons and appreciate other cultures. To give an example, the first time I tried Ethiopian food, I learned about sharing a meal from a communal platter, which taught me something meaningful about hospitality in that culture. It was so much more than just a tasty dinner. Beyond that, stepping out of your comfort zone keeps life interesting and can lead to wonderful surprises. Plenty of dishes I now love, like sushi and Thai curry, were ones I was once nervous to order. Some flavours are an acquired taste, but you will never discover a new favourite if you always order the same thing. Of course, I understand that some people find comfort in familiar dishes, and there is nothing wrong with having go-to meals you can rely on. In short, while sticking to the familiar is perfectly understandable, I firmly believe that being a little more adventurous with food enriches your life, expands your palate, and deepens your respect for other cultures.

title: Is Planning Meals In Advance Worth It
prompt_text: Some people carefully plan their meals for the entire week ahead, while others prefer to decide what to eat spontaneously, day by day. Do you think planning meals in advance is worth the time and effort? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Take a position on whether meal planning is worth it, give two reasons with examples, weigh the appeal of spontaneity, and finish with a decisive conclusion.
sample_response_band12: I'm of the opinion that planning meals in advance is well worth the effort for most busy people, even if it does take some discipline. To start with, planning ahead saves a remarkable amount of time and money. A perfect illustration is making a single grocery list for the whole week. You shop once, you buy only what you need, and you dramatically reduce food waste because nothing rots forgotten at the back of the fridge. What's more, meal planning makes it far easier to eat healthily. When you've already decided on balanced meals, you're much less likely to cave in and order greasy takeout after a long, exhausting day. I've found that just having a plan removes that tired evening question of what on earth to cook. Now, while it's true that some people value spontaneity and enjoy deciding on a whim, I'd argue that's a trade-off worth making. You can always leave one flexible night in the week. Ultimately, I firmly believe that the benefits of planning ahead, namely saving time, cutting waste, and eating better, far outweigh the small loss of spontaneity, which is exactly why I stand by it so strongly.

title: Strict Versus Relaxed Parenting
prompt_text: Some people believe that strict parenting, with clear rules and firm discipline, helps children grow into responsible adults. Others think a more relaxed approach, which gives children freedom to make their own choices, works better. Which approach do you think is more effective? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State which approach you prefer, give reason one with a concrete example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the strengths of the other approach, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, a relaxed approach generally works better than strict parenting, though it has to include some structure. First of all, when children are given the freedom to make their own choices, they learn to think for themselves. For instance, my younger cousin was allowed to choose his own hobbies and manage his own homework schedule, and as a result he became remarkably independent and self-motivated. Secondly, a relaxed approach builds trust between parents and kids. Children who aren't afraid of harsh punishment tend to be more honest with their parents, which means problems get solved earlier. On the other hand, I do recognize that strict parenting can instill discipline and clear boundaries, which some children genuinely need, especially when they're very young. However, I believe constant control can backfire and lead to rebellion or anxiety later on. All things considered, I firmly believe that a warm, relaxed approach, combined with a few firm rules, raises children who are confident, responsible, and capable of making good decisions on their own.

title: Paying Children For Chores
prompt_text: Some families pay their children an allowance for completing household chores such as washing dishes or cleaning their rooms. Other people believe chores are simply part of family life and should not be rewarded with money. Do you think children should be paid for doing household chores? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether you agree or disagree, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your conclusion.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I don't think children should be paid for doing everyday household chores. To begin with, chores are a shared responsibility, and kids should understand that everyone in a family has to contribute. For example, when I was growing up, my brother and I were expected to set the table and take out the garbage simply because we lived in the house, and that taught us teamwork rather than the idea that help should always come with a price. In addition, paying for chores can create a sense of entitlement, where children refuse to lift a finger unless money is involved. However, I do see some value in the other view, because a small allowance can teach kids how to budget and save. In that case, I'd suggest tying money to extra tasks, like washing the car, rather than basic daily duties. To sum up, I firmly believe that ordinary chores should be done out of responsibility, while occasional payment for bigger jobs can still teach valuable lessons about money.

title: Competitive Sports For Children
prompt_text: Some people believe that competitive sports are good for children because they build teamwork, discipline, and physical fitness. Others worry that the pressure to win can cause stress and harm a child's confidence. Do you think competitive sports are good for children? Give reasons and examples to support your view.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the concern about pressure, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that competitive sports are good for children, as long as they're managed in a healthy way. First and foremost, sports teach kids valuable life skills like teamwork and discipline. Take, for example, a child who plays on a hockey team; they quickly learn that the group only succeeds when everyone cooperates and shows up to practice. Secondly, competition builds resilience. Children learn how to handle losing gracefully and how to bounce back, which is a skill they'll need throughout their entire lives. What's more, regular sport keeps kids physically active and healthy at a time when many spend hours staring at screens. Admittedly, I do understand the worry that too much pressure to win can hurt a child's confidence or cause unnecessary stress. However, I think the solution is good coaching and supportive parents, not removing competition altogether. In conclusion, I firmly believe that competitive sports, when balanced with encouragement and fun, help children grow into healthier, more confident, and more cooperative individuals.

title: Limiting Children's Screen Time
prompt_text: Some parents set strict limits on how much time their children spend on phones, tablets, and computers. Others believe children should be allowed to manage their own screen time and learn self-control. Do you think parents should strictly limit their children's screen time? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State your position, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the opposing argument, then restate your view.
sample_response_band12: In my view, parents should set reasonable limits on their children's screen time, especially when the kids are young. First of all, devices can be genuinely addictive, and children often don't have the self-control to stop on their own. For instance, my neighbour's son would happily play video games for six hours straight if no one stepped in, and his sleep and homework clearly suffered. Secondly, too much screen time takes away from healthier activities like outdoor play, reading, and spending time with family. Furthermore, excessive scrolling can affect a child's mood and attention span. On the other hand, I do agree that children eventually need to learn how to manage their own time, and that constant control isn't realistic as they get older. So rather than banning screens completely, I think parents should set clear daily limits and gradually give kids more freedom as they mature. All in all, I firmly believe that sensible limits, combined with teaching self-control, are the healthiest approach for growing children.

title: Teaching Independence Early
prompt_text: Some people believe children should be taught to be independent at an early age, doing things like dressing themselves and making simple decisions on their own. Others feel young children need close guidance and protection rather than independence. Do you think children should be taught independence at an early age? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the value of guidance, then restate your conclusion.
sample_response_band12: From my perspective, children should definitely be taught independence at an early age, as long as the tasks are age-appropriate. To start with, learning to do small things on their own builds confidence. A good example of this is letting a five-year-old choose their own clothes or pour their own cereal; these little wins make children feel capable and proud. Secondly, early independence develops decision-making skills that become essential later in life. When kids are allowed to make small choices and even small mistakes, they learn to think for themselves rather than relying on others for everything. On top of that, independent children tend to adapt more easily to new situations, like starting school. Admittedly, very young children still need plenty of guidance, protection, and emotional support, and I'm not suggesting we leave them to figure everything out alone. The key is to balance freedom with supervision. Ultimately, I firmly believe that teaching independence early, in small and safe steps, raises self-sufficient and confident children who are far better prepared for the challenges of adulthood.

title: Paying For Good Grades
prompt_text: Some parents reward their children with money for getting good grades, believing it motivates them to study harder. Others think this teaches children to study only for the reward rather than for learning itself. Do you think rewarding good grades with money is a good idea? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State whether you support the idea, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: Honestly, I don't think rewarding good grades with money is a good idea in the long run. The main reason is that it teaches children to study for the cash rather than for the joy of learning. To illustrate, a friend of mine was paid for every A he got in high school, and once he reached university where no one was paying him, he completely lost his motivation to work hard. Another point is that money rewards can create unhealthy pressure and even tempt some kids to cheat just to earn the prize. In my view, children should understand that the real reward for studying is knowledge and future opportunities. However, I do see why parents do it, because a small incentive can genuinely encourage a struggling child to put in more effort, at least temporarily. To me, praising effort and curiosity matters far more than handing out cash. To conclude, I firmly believe that while money might work as a short-term push, building a real love of learning is far more valuable and lasting for a child's future.

title: Homeschooling Versus Regular School
prompt_text: Some families choose to homeschool their children, believing it allows for personalized learning and a safer environment. Others believe attending a regular school is better because it offers social interaction and a structured education. Which do you think is better, homeschooling or attending a regular school? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State which option you prefer, give reason one with an example, give reason two with an example, acknowledge the benefits of the other option, then restate your position.
sample_response_band12: As far as I'm concerned, attending a regular school is generally better than homeschooling for most children. First and foremost, regular schools offer crucial social interaction. For example, when kids spend their days surrounded by classmates from different backgrounds, they naturally learn how to make friends, resolve conflicts, and work in teams, which are skills that are hard to replicate at the kitchen table. In addition to that, regular schools provide a structured curriculum taught by trained teachers across many subjects, so children get a well-rounded education and access to facilities like science labs and sports fields. Granted, I understand the appeal of homeschooling, because it allows for personalized learning at the child's own pace and can feel safer for some families. In certain situations, such as a child with special needs, homeschooling might genuinely be the better choice. However, for the majority of children, I believe the social and academic benefits of a regular school far outweigh learning at home. All things considered, I firmly believe a regular school better prepares children for both their careers and real life.

title: Money Or Happiness As Success
prompt_text: Some people believe that success should be measured by how much money a person earns. Others think that success is really about how happy and satisfied a person feels with their life. Which do you think is the better way to measure success? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State which measure you favour, give reason one with a personal or real example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the value of the other side, then restate your position firmly.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, success is best measured by happiness rather than by money. First of all, money can make life comfortable, but it doesn't guarantee a meaningful life. For instance, I have an uncle who earns a very high salary, yet he works such long hours that he rarely sees his family, and honestly, he seems exhausted and unhappy most of the time. To me, that doesn't look like success at all. What's more, happiness tends to last, while money comes and goes. A friend of mine took a lower-paying teaching job, and even though she earns less, she feels proud and satisfied every single day because she's making a real difference. On the other hand, I do understand that financial security matters, since it's hard to feel happy when you're constantly worried about paying your bills. Still, money should be a tool for a good life, not the goal itself. All things considered, I firmly believe that a person who feels fulfilled and content is far more successful than someone who is simply wealthy but miserable.

title: Is Failure Necessary For Success
prompt_text: Some people say that failure is a necessary part of becoming successful, because we learn the most from our mistakes. Others believe that constant failure only discourages people and holds them back. Do you agree that failure is a necessary part of success? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Take a clear side, support it with reason one and a concrete example, add reason two with an example, concede a limited point to the other view, then conclude by restating your stance.
sample_response_band12: I firmly believe that failure is a necessary part of success, because it teaches us lessons that success simply can't. To begin with, when things go wrong, we're forced to stop and figure out what we did incorrectly. For example, the first time I gave a presentation at work, I froze and forgot half my points. It was embarrassing, but it pushed me to prepare far more carefully, and my next presentation went really well. Moreover, failure builds resilience. People who have never struggled often give up the moment something gets hard, whereas those who have bounced back from setbacks develop the toughness to keep going. Think of almost any successful athlete or entrepreneur, and you'll find a long list of failures behind them. In contrast, I admit that repeated failure with no progress can be discouraging, so it's important to actually learn from each mistake rather than just repeating it. In conclusion, failure isn't the opposite of success at all; it's one of the most important steps on the road toward it.

title: Ambition Versus Contentment
prompt_text: Some people think it is better to be ambitious and always strive for more in life. Others believe it is healthier to be content with what you already have. Which approach do you think leads to a better life? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose ambition or contentment, justify it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, recognize a strength of the opposing view, then restate your choice clearly.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I think it's better to be ambitious and keep striving for more, although it has to be balanced. The main reason is that ambition gives life direction and energy. To illustrate, a close friend of mine wasn't satisfied with her ordinary job, so she studied at night for two years and eventually became a nurse. That ambition completely transformed her life and gave her a real sense of purpose. On top of that, ambitious people tend to keep growing and learning, while those who are too content can become stuck and even a little bored over time. Having said that, I do recognize the value of contentment, because constantly chasing more can lead to stress and burnout if you never pause to appreciate what you have. So the ideal, in my view, is to stay ambitious while still feeling grateful along the way. To sum up, I believe a healthy ambition pushes us to reach our full potential, and that makes life richer than simply settling for what we already have.

title: Is Honesty Always the Best Policy
prompt_text: There is a common saying that honesty is always the best policy. However, some people argue that there are times when a small lie can be kinder or more helpful than the truth. Do you agree that honesty is always the best policy? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State whether you fully agree or partly agree, back it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, fairly acknowledge the exception, then restate your overall position.
sample_response_band12: I'd have to say that honesty is the best policy most of the time, though not in absolutely every situation. First and foremost, honesty builds trust, and trust is really the foundation of every relationship. Take, for example, a friendship: if someone discovers you've lied to them, even about something small, it becomes very hard for them to believe you again, and that damage can last for years. Another thing is that the truth usually works out better in the long run, because lies tend to grow and require even more lies to cover them up, which eventually falls apart. That said, I do think there's room for small, kind white lies. If a friend asks whether I like their new haircut, I'm not going to crush them with brutal honesty over something so minor. Overall, though, those gentle exceptions are pretty rare. In almost every important situation, I firmly believe that being truthful protects both your relationships and your own integrity far better than dishonesty ever could.

title: Lifelong Learning of New Skills
prompt_text: Some people believe that adults should keep learning new skills throughout their lives. Others feel that once people finish their education and settle into a career, there is no real need to keep learning. Do you think people should continue learning new skills throughout life? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason one with an example, add reason two with an example, briefly note the opposing view, then close by restating your opinion.
sample_response_band12: I strongly believe that people should keep learning new skills throughout their lives. One reason is that the world is changing incredibly quickly, especially with technology, so we have to keep learning just to stay relevant. As an example, my mother recently took an online course to learn new software at her job, and it completely changed how confident she feels at work. In addition, learning new things keeps the mind active and sharp. A neighbour of mine started learning the guitar in his sixties, and he says it gives him real joy and a sense of accomplishment every week. On the other side, I understand that some people feel they've already learned enough and just want to relax after years of studying, which is fair. However, even small steps, like picking up a hobby or a new language, can broaden your horizons without much pressure. All in all, I'm convinced that lifelong learning keeps us adaptable, mentally healthy, and engaged with the world around us.

title: Planned Life Versus Spontaneous Life
prompt_text: Some people prefer to live a carefully planned life, organizing their goals and schedules in advance. Others believe a more spontaneous life, where you go with the flow and make decisions in the moment, is more enjoyable. Which kind of life do you think is better? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: Pick the planned or spontaneous side, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, give credit to the other approach, then restate your preference.
sample_response_band12: From my point of view, a carefully planned life is generally better, though I think the ideal leaves a little room for spontaneity. The first reason is that planning helps you actually achieve your goals. For instance, when I decided to save money for a trip abroad, I made a clear monthly budget and stuck to it, and within a year I had enough. If I'd simply gone with the flow, I'm certain I would have spent it all. What's more, planning reduces stress, because you're not constantly scrambling at the last minute. People who plan their week tend to feel far more in control. However, I do see the appeal of spontaneity, since some of the best memories come from unexpected moments, like deciding on the spur of the moment to visit a friend. So I always try to keep a little flexibility in my plans.
