In the end, though, I firmly believe a well-planned life gives you direction and peace of mind, while still leaving space to enjoy life's surprises.

title: Helping Others or Helping Yourself
prompt_text: Some people believe that focusing on helping others is the key to a happy and meaningful life. Others think you should focus on yourself first, taking care of your own needs and goals. Which approach do you think leads to a better life? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: State which focus you believe is better, support it with reason one and an example, add reason two with an example, acknowledge the value of the other side, then restate your opinion.
sample_response_band12: In my view, focusing on helping others leads to a more meaningful and rewarding life, as long as you don't completely forget about yourself. To start with, helping others gives life a strong sense of purpose. A good example is my aunt, who volunteers at a local food bank every weekend. She always says it's the most fulfilling part of her week, and you can genuinely see how happy it makes her. Besides that, generosity tends to come back to you. When you support the people around you, they're far more likely to support you in return, which creates stronger friendships and a real sense of community. Then again, I do think a certain amount of self-care is essential, because you simply can't pour from an empty cup. If you neglect your own health and needs, you won't have the energy to help anyone at all. So balance does matter. To wrap up, though, I firmly believe that a life centred on helping others is richer and more satisfying than one focused only on yourself.

title: Should Public Transit Be Free
prompt_text: Some people believe that public transit, such as buses and trains, should be completely free for everyone. Others think that passengers should keep paying a fare to help cover the costs. Do you agree or disagree that public transit should be free? Give reasons and examples to support your opinion.
template_hint: State your position clearly, give reason one with a concrete example, give reason two with an example, briefly acknowledge the opposing view, then restate your opinion in a strong closing sentence.
sample_response_band12: In my opinion, public transit should be free for everyone, and I feel quite strongly about this. First of all, free transit would make cities far more affordable for ordinary people. For instance, a single parent who spends a hundred dollars a month on bus passes could put that money toward groceries or rent instead. Secondly, it would encourage more people to leave their cars at home, which would cut down on traffic congestion and pollution. I've noticed that whenever my city offers free transit days, the buses are packed and the roads are noticeably quieter. Now, I do understand the other side. Running a transit system is expensive, and someone has to pay for it, so taxes might have to go up a little. However, I think that's a worthwhile trade-off, because cleaner air and lower living costs benefit everyone in the long run. All things considered, free public transit is a smart investment that would make our cities healthier, fairer, and easier to live in, so I firmly believe it's the right way to go.

title: More Bike Lanes Or Car Lanes
prompt_text: Many cities are deciding whether to add more bike lanes, even when this means reducing the number of lanes available for cars. Some people support this change, while others worry it will make driving more difficult. Do you think cities should add more bike lanes even if it means fewer car lanes? Explain your opinion with reasons and examples.
template_hint: Choose a side and state it, support it with a first reason and example, add a second reason and example, acknowledge the drivers' concern, then conclude by reaffirming your stance.
sample_response_band12: Personally, I believe cities should add more bike lanes, even if it means giving up some car lanes. To begin with, dedicated bike lanes make cycling much safer, and when people feel safe, more of them choose to ride. For example, after my neighbourhood added a protected lane, I started biking to work, and I see dozens of others doing the same every morning. In addition, fewer cars on the road means cleaner air and less noise, which improves life for everyone, not just cyclists. In some downtown areas, you can actually notice the difference in air quality once traffic drops. That being said, I do sympathize with drivers who feel squeezed into narrower roads during rush hour. However, in the long run, encouraging cycling actually reduces traffic, because every person on a bike is one fewer car in the queue, so drivers may benefit too. To sum up, while there is a short-term trade-off, I am convinced that prioritizing bike lanes is the smarter, healthier choice for a growing city, and that is why I strongly support it.

title: Stricter Limits on Residential Noise
prompt_text: Some people feel that there should be stricter limits on noise in residential areas, such as rules against loud music or construction at night. Others believe that current rules are already enough and that more restrictions would be unfair. Do you agree or disagree that noise limits in residential areas should be stricter? Give reasons and examples for your opinion.
template_hint: Declare your opinion up front, back it with one reason and a real-life example, add a second supporting reason with an example, recognize the opposing argument, and end with a firm restatement.
sample_response_band12: I'm of the view that noise limits in residential areas should definitely be stricter. First and foremost, people need quiet to rest properly, and a good night's sleep is essential for health and work. To give an example, my upstairs neighbour used to play loud music until two in the morning, and I would show up to work exhausted and unable to concentrate. Stricter rules would protect people from situations like that. Furthermore, peaceful neighbourhoods are simply better places to raise children and care for the elderly, who are especially sensitive to constant noise. A calm environment really does improve everyone's quality of life. On the flip side, I understand that some people worry about being penalized for normal activities, like a weekend birthday party, and nobody wants overly harsh rules. However, sensible limits, such as a reasonable curfew on loud noise after eleven at night, would strike a fair balance. In conclusion, I firmly believe that stricter and well-enforced noise rules would make our neighbourhoods healthier and more pleasant, while still allowing people to enjoy themselves at reasonable hours.
